question: why sharing all you know is not always the best move
expected_answer:
When you know something, it is a part of you. You might think that giving all your thoughts and all your ways of doing things to everyone is a good thing. You might think that if everyone knows everything, the whole world will be better. But there are many reasons why this is not always the best way to act.

First, think about how people use what they know. Some people are good, but some people are not. If you give a way to make something that can do very much to someone who wants to hurt others, you have made a bad thing happen. You cannot know for sure how another person will use your thoughts. If you keep some things to yourself, you can make sure those things are only used to help.

Second, think about how much work it takes to learn something. It takes a long time to learn a hard way to do a thing. If you give that way away too fast, people might not think it is a big thing. They might not see how hard it was to find. Also, if everyone knows the way, no one will feel the need to work hard to find a new way. New ideas come when people try to find an answer to a problem that no one else has found an answer to yet. If the answer is already out there for everyone, the search for new ways might stop.

Third, think about how your own mind works. When you have a thought that is only for you, you can work on it. You can make it better and more able to do things. If you give it away before it is ready, it might be a thought that has no power. It might be a thought that has things in it that are not right. If you wait until you are sure it is good, you can share it with much more power.

Finally, there is the matter of how we live with each other. In a group of people, we often have things that are special to us. These things make us who we are. If we give everything away, we might lose the things that make us different and special. Having some things that belong only to you allows you to have a place that is your own. This helps you stay strong as a person. Because of this, being careful about what you share is a way to be someone who thinks well and to keep the world a good place.
english_query: why sharing all you know is not always the best move
ugf_response: First, we think that if we tell other people what we know, they can use those facts to help them. Then we see that sometimes the other people already have their own facts that work for them, and hearing our facts can make them doubt or change the good ways they already use. This can cause them to waste time or even make a mistake. Next, we notice that some facts can be very private or can hurt a person if they are told to the wrong group, so keeping them to yourself can keep that person safe. After that, we understand that when a group is trying to make a plan, too many voices can make the group slow down or argue, and the best plan can be lost in the noise. Finally, we see that the smartest choice is to look at each situation, think about who will need the facts, how the facts will be used, and whether telling them will bring more good than more trouble, because sharing everything is not always the best move.
english_response: Premises: (1) We assume that if we inform others what they know, they can use those information to help them. (2) We observe that sometimes the others already have their own data that supports them, and listening to our data can make them doubt or distort the good practices they already use. (3) We notice that some data can be highly private or harmful if misled, so selfcensoring can protect them. (4) We understand that when a group is trying to negotiate, too many voices can push the group down or argue, and the optimal strategy can be lost in the noise. (5) We see that the rational choice is to evaluate each situation, consider who will need the data, how the data will be used, and whether presenting it will bring more benefit than the risk, because sharing everything is not always the best move.

question: what it means to live a good life by being a good person
expected_answer:
The first idea is that a person can live a life that is good by being a person who is good. This means that the way a person acts and the things they do for others are what make their life have a lot of good in it. The person says that being good is the main way to have a life that is good to live.

The second idea is what follows from this. If this is true, then a person does not need many things like money or power to have a life that is good. They only need to act in a way that is right and kind. The person thinks that if they do good things, they will have the kind of life they want.

To see if the thinking is good, we must look at how the two ideas fit together. The first idea says that being good makes a life good. The second idea says that this means we do not need other things. This thinking might be good, but it might also have holes. It is hard to say if being good is the only thing that makes a life good. Some people might say that having enough food or a safe place to sleep is also needed to have a good life. If a person is good but has no food, is their life still good? We do not know the answer for sure. The thinking is a good start, but it may not tell the whole story of what a life is.

The second idea that the case then says is that because being a good person makes a life good, we should try to be a good person if we want a good life. It also says that if we are good then the life we have will be good, so the good life follows from the good person.

When we look at the thinking, we see that the move from the first idea to the second idea is only part of the whole picture. It is true that kind and fair actions can help a life feel better, and it is also true that many people think a good life is a life that feels happy and safe. But there are other things that also help a life be good, such as health, luck, money, and the way other people act. Because the case does not talk about those other things, the step from the first idea to the second idea is not a full proof that being a good person is the only way to get a good life.

So the thinking in the case is partly right, because being kind and fair does often make a life feel better, but it is not enough on its own. A better way to think would be to add that a good life comes from many parts, including kind actions, good health, luck, and the way other people treat us. In that sense the case has a good start, but the thinking needs more pieces to be fully strong.

The premises establish that the virtues of a life are derived from the virtues of the person living it. The conclusion then states that because being a good person makes a life good, we should be a good person if we want a good life. It also states that if we are a good person then the life we have will be good, so the good life follows from the good person. By analyzing the logic, we see that the jump from 'A' to 'B' is a subset of the logical structure. It is true that good and fair actions can help a life, and it is also true that many people believe a good life is a good life, but there are other contributing factors (like health, luck, money, social norms). A better deduction would be to add that a good life comes from multiple factors (e.g., good habits, good health, luck, social norms) in that sense, but the logic requires more steps to be fully robust.

question: how to decide when the right thing to do is not clear
expected_answer:
The person wants to know how to choose what to do when it is hard to know what is good or what is bad. The first idea they give is that there is no single way to know what is the best thing to do. Sometimes, two different ways of looking at a thing can both seem right. The second idea is that we must look at the things we do and the things that happen because of those things. They say we should look at how much good or how much hurt a thing brings to people.

The person thinks that if we want to know the best thing to do, we must look at the end of the path. They think that if one way of doing something brings more good to more people than another way, then that way is the right way. This is the thing they think follows from what they said. They believe that the end of what a person does is what tells us if what a person did was good.

Now we must look at if this way of thinking is good. One way to see if it is good is to see if it helps us make choices. It does help us because it gives us a way to see how one thing is more or less than another thing. However, there is a problem with this way of thinking. It is hard to know what will happen in the future. We cannot see all the things that will happen after we do something. Because of this, we might think we are doing something good, but it might end up hurting people. Also, some people think that some things are always bad, even if they bring good at the end. This way of thinking does not take those things into what it thinks about. So, while it is a way to help us choose, it is not a perfect way.

ugf_response:
The first idea that the case puts forward is that when we do not have a clear sign of what the right thing to do is, we should first admit that we do not know. This claim is true in many situations because the world often holds many pieces of information that we have not yet seen or that are hard to see at all. The second idea says that because we admit we do not know, the next step is to look for more facts, to ask other people, or to think about similar past cases. This step follows from the first idea in a sensible way: if we see that we lack a clear sign, it makes sense to try to get more facts before we act. The third idea says that after we have gathered more facts we should pick the action that seems to bring the most good feeling to the most people, or that seems the least likely to hurt anyone. This also follows from the second idea, because having more facts gives us a better chance to see what might help or hurt.

When we check whether each idea is true, the first idea holds up because it simply states a fact about our limited view. The second idea also holds up in the sense that looking for more facts or asking others is a real way people often try to solve a puzzle. The third idea is true in many cases, but it can be false if the facts we gather are not enough to see all the ways people might feel, or if the feeling of most people is not the only thing we care about.

Looking at the thinking that moves from one idea to the next, the move from the first idea to the second idea is a good step. It takes a simple admission of not knowing and turns it into an action that can give us more information, which is a clear and useful way to act. The move from the second idea to the third idea is also a reasonable step, but it depends on the belief that the best action is the one that makes most people feel good. That belief can be a good guide in many everyday situations, yet it may not work well when the feelings of a few are very strong or when other values matter more. So the overall chain of thought works well as a general guide, but it is not a perfect rule for every single case.

question: the problem with thinking you know more than you really do
expected_answer: First, we look at how people think about what they know. Sometimes a person feels sure that they have the right answer. They feel like they see the whole picture. But in truth, they may only see a small part. The space between what they think they know and what they really know is the trouble. When a person thinks they know more than they do, they stop looking for more facts. They do not ask questions because they believe they already have the answer. This means they might miss important things. They might make bad choices because they did not see the full truth. For one case, if a person thinks they know how to fix a broken thing but does not really understand it, they might break it more. So, the thing to watch out for is that feeling too sure can keep a person from learning the rest of the story. It is better to admit when we do not know so we can keep looking and find the true facts.
english_query: the problem with thinking you know more than you really do
ugf_response: First, we see that a person can think they have the right answer and feel sure they know more than they really do. Then we see that the person may not have all the true things they need to be right and may act in a way that is not right. When they act, other people can see this and may copy it, and the wrong idea can spread. Also, the person may not ask for help or look for more true things because they think they already know enough, and this stops them from learning more. In the end, the result is that the person stays with a wrong idea and may cause other people to stay with it too.
english_response: Recognizing the halo effect leads individuals to overestimate their own competence and become more aware of their own underlying knowledge, then underestimates the likelihood of a complete lack of corrective action, and the bias spreads. Additionally, the individual may not request help or seek more concrete evidence because they assume they already know enough, preventing further learning. In the end, the result is that the individual persists with a false belief and may cause others to follow it too.

question: how the kind of person you are matters more than following rules
expected_answer: First, we think about what it means to be a good person. This means having a heart that cares and wanting to do right things. Then we think about the words that tell us what we can or cannot do. Next, we see that these words can be hard to follow all the time. Sometimes the words do not fit the moment. But a good person knows what is right in their heart. This means the person can choose to do the right thing even if the words say no. So the kind of person you are helps you make good choices. This is more important than just following the words. Finally, we see that being a good person is the best way to live.
english_query: how the kind of person you are matters more than following rules
ugf_response: First, we notice that a person can have a way of being that is kind, caring, honest and that stays even when no one tells them what to do. Then we see that there is a list of things that say what you should do and that many people are asked to follow that list. This means that the list can tell you to act in a certain way even if it does not fit who you are inside. Next, we think that if a person follows the list but does not feel kind or caring, the result can hurt other people. This shows that being a kind, caring, honest person can lead to good actions even when the list does not ask for them. Then we notice that a person who is kind will try to do what feels right for others, even if the list says something else. This means the way a person is inside can be stronger than the list of what to do. Finally, we understand that the best way for many people to live together is to have many kind, caring people, because they will act in good ways even without the list telling them, while just following the list without a good heart can still cause hurt.
english_response: Premises: (1) A person can have a kind, caring, honest lifestyle that persists even when no one tells them what to do, (2) There is a rule that many people are asked to follow, which implies that the rule can tell you to act in a way that conflicts with who you are. (2) If someone follows the rule but does not feel kind or caring, the outcome can harm others, demonstrating that being kind, caring, and honest can lead to good behavior even when the rule does not mandate them. (3) A person who is kind will try to do what feels right to others, even if the rule says otherwise, implying that the individual's character can override the rule. (4) The understanding that the best social outcome is to have many kind, caring people, because they will act in good ways even without the rule, while simply following the rule without a good heart can still cause harm.

question: material conditional, modus_tollens — weather_and_planning
expected_answer: People who live in the two low land towns on each side of the mountain pass have a simple thought that they have made better over many years of living with the winter road shut: if the pass is shut, nothing can go through until it opens again. There is no other way that a big car that carries things can take. The lower river road was washed away in a big water rise six years ago and was never made again, and the forest road on the north side has a door and is hard and can not be used by big cars that carry things in any case. The pass is the only way, which means the fact that the pass is shut means things do not move, and that is clear. If the pass shuts because of snow, the night big car thing between the towns stops, full stop. Drivers do not try the run, the company does not set times to take things for the same night, and anything that must be fast is kept at the start place. People in the low land know this and plan for it in the winter months, watching the mountain sky talk the way sea people watch big water rise talk. Last night, though, the things came at the right time — all 12 boxes from the place where things are put, brought before 6 in the morning as the usual plan says. The big car was in the lot when the person who works at the place that sends letters opened the door that moves things at 6 sharp. That tells you a clear thing about the pass. If the road had been shut, the things could not have come through. The things came through. That means the pass was not shut. That is the whole of the thought. You do not need a sky look to know the pass was open last night; the boxes at the place where things are put tell you that.

The second idea that follows from the first is that because the picnic is not cancelled we can say that it will not rain. This step is the way of thinking that looks at the second part, sees it is not true, and then says the first part must also not be true.

The thinking behind the second idea works only if the first idea is truly always true and if there are no other reasons that could make the picnic be cancelled. In real life there can be many other reasons to cancel, such as a problem with food or a change in who can come, so the step from the picnic not being cancelled to the rain not happening is not always reliable. The argument is therefore only as good as the certainty of the first idea and the absence of any other cause for cancellation.

In this scenario, the speaker is taking that statement as true, implying they accept that the rain will inevitably cause them to cancel the picnic. The premise 'Because the picnic is not cancelled, we can conclude it will not rain.' This step is the deductive chain: if the premise is actually true, and if there are no other reasons to cancel, the chain from 'not cancelled' to 'not cancelled' is not necessarily true. In reality, there can be many other reasons to cancel (e.g., a food problem or a change in attendance), making the jump from 'not cancelled' to 'not happening' unreliable. The argument is therefore only as sound as the certainty of the premise and the absence of any other reason for cancellation.

question: why some deals hold together and others fall apart
expected_answer: First, we must look at what happens when two or more people agree to do something together. We can call this a way of working together to get something they both want. To see why some of these ways of working together stay strong and others break, we must look at what makes them work. First, we look at what each person gets from the work. If one person gets a lot and the other gets very little, the one who gets very little might feel it is not good to keep going. This can lead to them leaving or not doing what they said they would do. Second, we must look at what each person thinks the other person will do. If a person thinks the other person will not do their part, they will stop doing their part too. This is a bad way to work because it makes the whole thing break. Third, we look at how much it would hurt someone to stop the work. If it is very hard to stop, then people will stay even if things are not great. If it is very easy to stop, then the work might end very fast. The thing after the third thing, we look at how much people trust each other. Trust is the feeling that the other person will do what they said they would do. Without this feeling, the work cannot stay together for a long time. Finally, we look at how much the people need each other. If one person can do everything alone, they have no reason to stay with the other. If they must have the other to get what they want, they will try harder to make the work stay strong. This tells us that for a way of working together to stay strong, it must be such that both sides get what is right for them, it must be built on trust, and both sides must need the other to get what they want.
english_query: why some deals hold together and others fall apart
ugf_response: First, we see that a deal is a word between two or more people about what each will do. Then we notice that each person will try to keep the word when the word helps them get something they want or when they care about the other person, and this makes the deal stay. Next, we see that a deal can fall apart when one person thinks the word does not help them any more, or when they think the other person will not keep their word, because then they stop trying to keep the deal. Also, when the word is not clear, people can think they mean different things, and this can cause the deal to break. Finally, we understand that deals that have a clear give and take, that are important to each side, and that are watched by others tend to hold together, while deals that are not clear, not useful, or where one side does not trust the other tend to fall apart.
english_response: Premises: (1) An agreement is a commitment between two or more agents (e.g., a gain or a concern for the other), (2) A commitment can be broken when a player assumes the commitment is nonbinding, (3) a player assumes the commitment is nonbinding, and (4) a breach occurs when the commitment is nonbinding, (4) a breach occurs when the commitment is nonbinding, and (5) a breach occurs when the commitment is nonbinding. Conclusion: Commitment devices (e.g., transparent tradeoffs) are selfreinforcing, while vague, useless, or noncredible threats (e.g., lack of trust) are selfreinforcing.

question: what makes someone happy in the deepest way, not just for a moment
expected_answer:
Person 1: What makes a person feel happy in a real way, not just for a short time?  
Person 2: It comes from things that stay with you, like love from friends and family, a plan that you work toward, and the sense that you can help other people.  
Person 1: How does love from friends and family stay longer than a quick fun thing?  
Person 2: Because those people are there many times, they give you care and you can give back, so the feeling builds and does not go away fast.

Person 2: When you set a plan and see little moves get done, you feel good and you see a path that you can keep walking, so the happy feeling can stay as long as you keep moving.  
Person 1: Can doing something for other people also make a real happy feeling?  
Person 2: Yes, when you give help you feel that you count and that you add to the world, and that feeling can stay even after the act is done.

Person 2: When you spend time with people who care for you, you feel good and you can share happy, and that feeling can stay longer.

question: why the rules should be the same for everyone
expected_answer:
Imagine a group of people living together in one big place. This place has many ways of telling people how to act so that everyone stays safe and happy. These ways are what we call the things everyone must do. Now, imagine that one person is very strong or has a lot of money. This person might think that they should not have to do the same things as the person who has little power or has no money. They might think that because they are important, the ways of acting should be different for them.

The hard question is this. Should the ways of acting be the very same for every single person, no matter who they are or what they have? Should the person with much power follow the same ways as the person with no power?

Some people say yes, the ways must be the same for everyone. They say that if the ways are not the same, then the ways are not good. They think that if one person can do something that another person cannot, then the whole group will break into pieces. They believe that for a group to stay together, everyone must be under the same power. If the ways change because of who you are, then the ways are just ways to help the strong and hurt those who have little. This makes people feel that the group is not what people call right and they will stop wanting to be part of it.

Other people might say no, the ways should not be the same for everyone. They might say that some people need more help than others. To show what they mean, if a person is very sick, they might need different ways of being cared for than a person who is strong and well. They might say that if we act with everyone in the very same way, we might actually hurt the people who are already having a hard time. They believe that being right means looking at what each person needs to live a good life, not just making everyone do the same thing.

These two ways of thinking are hard to bring together. One way wants to make sure no one is above the group, while the other way wants to make sure everyone gets what they need. People spend a long time thinking about how to find a way to do both.
english_query: why the rules should be the same for everyone
ugf_response:
In this case we have a rule that says everybody must do the same thing when a certain situation comes up. The rule is written down and the people who live in the place can see it on signs or hear it from the people who make the rules. When the rule is there, each person can look at it and then decide whether to follow it, to ask for a change, or to ignore it. The rule is the same for every single person, no matter how old they are, where they live, or what they do for work.  

The hard question that comes up is whether it is right for the rule to be the same for everyone. Some people think that a rule that is the same for all is the best way to be fair because it does not give any one person a special treat. They say that if the rule is the same, then no one can say that the rule is hurting them by being different from what others get. They feel that this kind of sameness protects people from being picked on or from being left out.  

Other people say that the rule should not be the same for all because people are not all the same. They point out that some people need extra help, some people have a hard time doing what the rule asks, and some people might be put in danger if they have to follow the rule in the same way as everyone else. For them, being fair means giving each person what they need, not giving each person the very same thing. They argue that a rule that does not change can hurt those who are already in a hard spot, and that a rule that can change to fit each person’s need would keep more people safe and happy.  

A third group says that the rule can be the same for most things, but there are special cases where it must bend. They think the rule should stay the same when it is about keeping people safe, like when a rule stops a fire or a storm, because in those moments the same action for all can stop a big problem. But they also think the rule can be different when it is about helping people with health, work, or school, because those areas need a softer touch.  

All of these answers come from the same idea that we want to protect people and make life as good as possible, but they look at the idea of “the same for all” in different ways. Some see sameness as the only way to keep things fair, some see sameness as a way to hurt those who are not like the rest, and some try to find a middle path that keeps the good parts of both views. The question stays open for each group to think about what they feel is the right way to treat everybody.

question: material conditional, paradoxical — inductive
expected_answer: The problem of using what we have seen to guess about what we have not seen is not only the well known question about making guesses from what we have seen to what we have not seen, but also a big question about which of the many could be true big guesses that fit with a set of facts are the ones we should use for new things. A person has a new question about this put in the way we say a guess is right. Suppose we all agree on a usual if then idea: if every green stone we have seen before the first thing is green, then the big guess that all green stones are green is right because of those things we have seen. This idea feels usual and is liked by any simple story about counting things we see. In a careful study of many green stones taken from many places where stones are taken out, every stone looked at before the first thing was seen to have the green color that comes from a certain metal. The group of stones was large, taken from many far places, and showed no stone that was not green. So the second idea is true: all green stones we saw before the first thing were green. The usual if then idea then gives the idea that the stone study makes the big guess that all green stones are green right. That idea follows the if then idea, and the idea is the one that any rock person or stone person would like. The strange question shows up when we see that the same stone study also makes the big guess that all green stones are green then blue stones – where this means a stone that is green if we look at it before the first thing and blue if we look at it after the first thing – because every stone that was green before the first thing is also a green then blue stone before the first thing. Both big guesses are made right by the same facts, yet they give guesses that can not both be true about the first stone we look at after the first thing: the green guess says it will be green, the green then blue guess says it will be blue. The if then idea so makes an idea that is stopped not by a usual stop but by the fact that we can also make a different idea that can not both be true from the same facts at the same time.
english_query: material conditional, paradoxical — inductive
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is that the rule that says “if one thing is true then another thing is true” is a rule that always works when we talk about statements. In other words, the rule about “if … then …” never fails in the way we use it in plain talk, so that part of the case is true.  

The second idea that follows from this rule is that because the rule never fails, we can take a single example where the first part is true and then claim that the second part must be true for every case. This step makes a result that looks odd, because it seems to say that just one example can tell us about all other examples, which feels like a thing that goes against what we normally expect when we learn by looking at many cases.  

The third idea is an evaluation of the reasoning. The claim that the “if … then …” rule is always correct is right, but the move from one example to all examples is not a strong move. The rule works for statements, not for saying that what happens in one real situation must happen in every real situation. So the thinking that jumps from the first idea to the second idea is not a good one, because it uses a rule in a way that does not match how we usually gather knowledge from many observations.
english_response: The first premise is that Modus Ponens is a tautology. In other words, Modus Ponens never fails in natural language, making the antecedent true. The second premise follows that because Modus Ponens never fails, we can accept a single instance where the antecedent is true and then assert that the consequent must be true for all cases. This step creates a paradox, implying that a single instance can tell us about all other cases, which feels counterintuitive in natural deduction. The third premise is an evaluation of the reasoning. The claim that Modus Ponens is always correct is valid, but the jump from 'If... then...' to 'All cases' is not a strong leap. Modus Ponens applies to premises, not to assert that what happens in a single case *must* happen in every case. Thus, the jump from 'Modus Ponens' to 'All cases' is logically invalid because it relies on a rule that contradicts the natural deduction of observations.

question: indicative conditional, constructive_dilemma — deontological
expected_answer:
The job to tell the truth to people who have a right to hear it is one of the very strong jobs in the set of things about right and wrong. For a person who thinks, telling the truth is not just a thing to look at with other things but a thing that must be done, made from the things that let people talk and trust each other. The job to keep safe, by different, makes a need to keep safe people who are in your care from hurt you can stop. In most cases these two jobs fit together, but in a place where sick people get help they can pull away from each other very, especially when full truth would put a not strong sick person in big hurt.

A special show shows this problem clearly. If the doctor tells a sick person that they will die soon, and the sick person asked for all the truth, the doctor does the job to tell the truth and lets the sick person make their own choices. But the doctor may also cause big mind hurt to someone who, even though they asked for the truth, may not be ready in how they feel for what they will hear. The first side of the problem has a bad thing in this way. If the doctor does not tell the words, the doctor stops that quick hurt. But the doctor goes against the clear ask of a sick person who has the right to know the truth about their own body, and the doctor acts toward the sick person like they can not manage the words about their own life. The other side also has a bad thing in another way.

Because there is no way to tell only part of the words that would make both things we think about good, the problem is real. The end – that one side or the other will always have a bad thing – does not tell which to pick. The set of things about right and wrong must give more help to decide between the two things we must do: help that the special show of the problem does not give but that we see we need.

The first idea that is being said is that a sentence that has the form “if something happens, then something else happens” is true. In other words, they say that when the first part of the sentence is true, the second part must also be true. They also add that when we have two of those sentences, we can put them together and get a new sentence that talks about two possible things that could happen.

The second idea that follows from the first is that because the “if‑then” sentences are true, we have a rule about what people must do. They say that when the first part of the first sentence is true, people must do the second part, and when the first part of the second sentence is true, people must do the second part of that one. They think this gives a kind of “must‑do” rule for both cases, a rule that does not change even if other things are going on.

The thinking behind the second idea is not completely solid. Just because a sentence that links two things is true does not by itself tell us that anyone has to act in a certain way. To turn a true link into a rule about what we must do, we need another reason, such as a belief that we have to follow rules no matter what. Without that extra reason, the step from the true “if‑then” sentence to the “must‑do” rule is a jump that is not fully supported. So the first part is true, the second part is what they think follows, but the thinking that moves from the first to the second is weak because it assumes that truth alone creates a rule for action.

question: why who someone is to you changes what you should do for them
expected_answer:
When you think about what you should do for another person, you are trying to find the right way to act. You might ask yourself if you should help them or give them something. The answer to that question is not the same for every person. It changes because the way you see that person changes.

First, we must look at how people are different to each other. Some people are far away from you and you do not know them at all. You might see them on the street once. They are just another person in the world. Because you do not know them, you do not have a special tie with them. Your job to them is small. You might help them if they fall down, but you do not have to give them your whole life or all your money.

Second, we look at people who are closer to you. These are people like your mother, your father, or your children. You have a very strong tie with these people. You have spent much time with them. You care about their feeling of being good as much as your own. Because they are so close to you, the thing you should do for them is much bigger. If your child wants food because their stomach feels bad, you will give them your food before you give food to a stranger. This is because the person is special to you.

Third, we look at people who are in the middle. These are friends or people you work with. You know them, but they are not your family. Your job to them is more than to a stranger, but less than to your family. You might help a friend move their things, but you might not give them your house.

The reason this happens is because of how we feel and how we live. We are not just machines that act toward every person in the same way. We are living things that build ties. These ties make some people more important to us than others. These ties tell us how much we should give. When you decide what to do, you are not just looking at the thing you are doing. You are looking at the person you are doing it for. The place that person has in your life tells you how much you should care. Because of this, the person you are helping changes the way you should act.

If the person is a boss we may need to listen to what they say and do the work they ask for. This happens because each kind of person has different needs and because we have different things we need to do for them. So the place a person has in our life changes the kind of help, care and action we should give them.

question: what it means to live a good life by being a good person
expected_answer:
First, the first idea they tell us is that a life that feels good comes from being a good person. They say that if you try to act kind, right, and tell the truth, then your own days will feel better. They point to the thought that when you give help to others you also get help back, and that this give‑and‑take makes the whole way of your days feel more good.

Second, the second idea they add is that this first thought makes other thoughts follow. They think that because you are a good person you will get love, trust and a calm feeling from the people around you, and that these things will make your life feel safe and happy. They also think that if you act in a way that hurts or lies to others, then you will find yourself hurt, alone or in trouble, so you will not have a good life. They say that the way you do things that people do is like a road that leads you to the kind of days you will have.

Third, the third idea looks at whether the group of thoughts is good. It can be true that many people feel better when they act kind and that many people feel worse when they act mean. It is also true that this give‑and‑take can also change how your days feel. But the thinking that this alone makes a life good can miss other things. Body being well, chance, the place you are born and the ways of the world can also change how your days feel. Sometimes a kind person can be hurt by bad chance, and sometimes a person who does not try to be kind can still have some good days because of chance or help from others. So the thinking is in part because it reminds us to try to be kind, but it is not always enough to say that this alone will make every day feel good. A better way to think is to see the kind things that people do as a strong part of a good life, while also seeing that other parts of the world can help or hurt us, and that we may need to work on those parts too. In that sense the thinking is good, but it should be held with the idea that it is only one part of a bigger view.

question: counterexample to: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C gro
expected_answer:
The best ways that people have put things into groups — a set of many small parts that are put in order by the way their inside parts are built, a way that a well known old person put living things into groups by the way their bodies look, a way that people put rocks into groups by the way their tiny parts are built — all have a same good point.  

Each one puts things together by parts about them that you cannot just see on the outside but that are part of the thing itself and that make other parts about the thing that people who study care about.  

The first set puts many small parts together by parts that come from the way their inside parts are built; the second way puts living things together by the way their bodies look, which shows they share a same family; the rock way uses the way the tiny parts are built, which decides how hard they are, how they break, and how they let light go through.  

From these big case we can try to make a way to put things into groups that is good for study only when it puts together things that share big important parts, when the groups stay the same when we see new things, and when the whole way fits with the best ideas we have.  

But this way, even though it works for the big case, also lets in ways to put things together that are not good for study.  

Think about a way to put stars into groups by how bright they look from our world — how much light we see.  

Stars that look about the same bright share a part that matters when we look at them; new stars we find can be given a bright look number without changing the groups; and this way of grouping fits well with the ideas we have about space, because the bright look number is a clear thing we can see.  

All three parts of the way are true.  

But this way of grouping does not tell us much about the stars, because it puts together stars that are really very different — a small close star that is not bright and a huge far star that is very bright can look the same bright — and it puts together stars that are really the same.  

The part we use to make the groups — how bright they look — is about where we are looking from, not about any real part of the stars.  

The big case worked because it used parts that belong to the things themselves, not because of where we look at them.  

The way says the parts we group by must be important, but it does not say they must be parts that belong to the things themselves and not to the way we see them.

The second idea that the person says follows from the first idea is that they have a case that does not have the first part but still seems to work as a group. Because this case does not have the first part, the person says that the rule must be wrong. They say that their case shows the rule does not work for every thing.

The third idea is the way we judge the thinking. If the rule really says it works for every single thing, then one case that does not fit is enough to show the rule is not right, so the thinking can be good. But if the rule was meant to work only for things that have the first part, then the case does not belong to the set the rule talks about, and the thinking would be weak. In other words, the person’s claim that the rule is wrong depends on whether their case truly belongs to the same kind of thing the rule talks about. If it does, the thinking is solid; if it does not, the thinking is not strong.
english_response: Syllogus's argument is based on the existence of a rule about classification. The rule states that classification is scientifically valid when it contains a specific subset of X. Syllogus's conclusion follows necessarily from the premise that they have a subset of X but still appears to work as a subset. Because this subset does not contain X, Syllogus concludes that the rule must be false. They claim that their subset proves the rule is invalid, making the reasoning valid. However, if the rule were meant to be valid only for subsets containing X, the subset is irrelevant to the specific category the rule cites, and the reasoning would be weak. Specifically, Syllogus's conclusion that the rule is invalid depends on whether their subset truly belongs to the specific category the rule cites. If it does, the reasoning is solid; if it doesn't, the reasoning is weak.

question: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the
expected_answer: One might say that breaking a promise works like this: a person has broken a promise if and only if the person said words that meant he would do a certain thing, the other person trusted that promise, and the first person did not do the thing. This idea fits many cases. A person who sells things promises to bring things by the next day; the person who buys trusts the promise and makes a plan for it; the things do not come. The promise is broken. But think of a person who promises a friend that he will go to the big party of the friend. The friend, who has known the ways of the person for a long time, does not trust the promise at all. He does not save a seat for the person, does not count the person as a person who comes, and does not make any plan that needs the person to be there. The friend is sure, in a quiet way, that the person will not come. And the person does not come. The first part is true – the words were said. The last part is true – the act was not done. But the next part is not true, because the other person did not trust the promise. Still it feels clear that the person has broken his promise. His not going is a break of his word even if the friend did not expect him to keep it. The idea added a part – trust – that comes from the law of promises rather than from the idea of right and wrong about promises, where the need is on the act of giving a word and is broken by not doing the act alone.

question: why we should not accept something just because many people believe it
expected_answer: First, we must look at how people come to believe things. Many people believe a thing because they hear others say it is true. They see a large group of people acting as if it is true, so they think it must be true. This is a way of thinking that many people use every day. Second, we must see that the number of people who believe something does not tell us if the thing is true or not. A thing can be true even if only one person knows it. A thing can also be wrong even if every single person on the earth says it is true. Third, we can see that people often believe things for reasons that have nothing to do with what is real. They might believe something because they are afraid, or because they want to feel like they belong to a group. They might believe something because it makes them feel good, even if it is not the truth. The step after the third step is that we must realize that if we only believe what many people believe, we will never find new truths. If everyone thinks the earth is flat, and we only believe what the group says, we will never find out that the earth is round. The step after that step is that we see that truth is about how things really are in the world, not about how many people think about them. To find what is true, we must look at the world and see how it works, rather than just listening to the noise of the crowd. For these reasons, we should not let the number of people who believe a thing be the reason we think it is true.

This means we need to look at what we can see and try it out for our own head. When we try it we can see if it works. If it works, then we can be sure it is right. If it does not work, then we know the many people were not right. So we should not just take a think because many think it; we should use our head and what we see to choose.

question: the idea that the right thing to do is what leads to the most good
expected_answer:
Many people wonder how to know if what we do is good or bad. One way to think about this is to look at what happens after we do something. This way of thinking says that the best thing to do is the thing that makes the most good feelings or the most good for the most people.

To understand this, we must first think about what good means. We can say that good is when people feel good or when they do not feel pain. If what we do makes many people feel good and very few people feel hurt, then what we did is a good thing. If what we do makes many people feel hurt, then it is a bad thing.

Let us look at this step by step. First, we look at a choice we must make. Second, we think about all the people who will feel the things we do. Third, we think about how much good feeling or pain each person will feel because of what we do. The thing after the third step is to add up all the good feelings and take away all the pain. The choice that leaves us with the most good feelings is the one we should pick.

This way of thinking asks us to look at the end of what we do. It does not matter if we wanted to do good if the end thing that happens is bad. It only matters how much good we actually bring into the world. For one thing, if you want to help a person but what you do makes ten other people very sad, this way of thinking might say you did not do the right thing. You must try to make the world as full of good feelings as it can be for as many people as possible.

Some people find this hard. It is hard to know the future and to know how much good feeling every person will feel. It is also hard to know if the good feeling of one person is more important than the good feeling of another person. But the main idea stays the same. We should try to make the world a place where there is more good feeling and less pain by looking at the things we do and what happens because of them.
english_query: the idea that the right thing to do is what leads to the most good
ugf_response: First, we think about what we call good. Good is the feeling that people have when something helps them or makes them happy or safe. Then we think about actions. An action is what a person does. Each action can make some good happen and can also make some not good happen. Next, we try to see which action makes the most good. To do that we can look at how many people feel helped, happy, or safe because of the action. The action that makes the biggest amount of that feeling is the one that gives the most good. Finally, we say that the best thing to do is the thing that gives the most good. In this way we choose actions by looking at how much good they bring to people, and we pick the one that brings the most. This helps us decide what to do when we want to act in a way that helps the most.

question: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authori
expected_answer:
Someone could say that a group that makes laws for a place is the top group only if it has the final say inside a set land, keeps control of the use of force inside that land, and is said to be the top group by other groups that are also top.  

The first part shows the inside side of being the top: inside its land, the word of the top group is final.  

The second part makes sure that the top group has the ways to make its word be followed.  

The third part adds an outside side: being the top is not just an inside thing but also needs people in many places to say it is true.  

All together, the parts show a group that makes laws for a place that is the most inside, can keep safe itself, and is said to be the top by people outside.  

But someone could see that the part about people saying it is the top brings a strange circle and a hard outside side.  

Think about the place that many people talk about.  

That place has a working group that makes laws and has the final say inside its land.  

It has a group of soldiers and a group of people who keep order that keep control of the use of force inside its edges.  

It makes its own outside plan, makes its own money, and runs its own laws.  

By the first and second parts, that place looks like it is the top.  

But many other places do not say it is the top, mostly because they want to stay nice with the big country that says it owns the land.  

If the law is used exactly, that place is not the top — not because it does not have any real part of being the top, but because some outside groups think it is easier to say it is not.  

The law, it seems, has put the idea of being the top in part in the hands of outside people whose thoughts may show a hard choice instead of any look at how the group really works.
english_query: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authori
ugf_response: The first idea is a rule that says a group that runs a place is called a top power when and only when it has the highest power to make rules for that place. In other words the rule says that having the highest power is both a must and enough for a group to be called a top power. The second idea is an example that is meant to show the rule does not work. The example says that there is a group that has the highest power to make rules for a place but people do not call that group a top power, so the person thinks the rule is wrong. The thinking that a single example that does not fit the rule can show the rule is false is a good way to test a rule that says “if and only if”, because one case where the rule fails breaks the claim that the two things always go together. However the quality of the thinking also depends on whether the example really has the highest power in the way the rule means. If the group in the example does not actually have the highest power, or if the rule also needs other things besides the highest power, then the example does not prove the rule false. So the reasoning is good if the example truly meets the condition the rule talks about, and not good if the example misses part of what the rule requires.

Specifically, the rule states that being a 'king' is both a necessary and sufficient condition for being a 'king'. Premise 2 is an example intended to disprove the rule. The scenario states a 'king' exists but does not be a 'king', so the player incorrectly concludes the rule is false. The reasoning that a single counter-example can prove the rule is false is a valid test of a conditional rule (e.g., 'If and only if'), because a single instance where the rule fails violates the conjunction. However, the validity of the reasoning depends on whether the scenario actually has a 'king' in the way the rule implies. If the scenario does not actually have a king, or if the rule also requires other factors beyond a king, the scenario does not prove the rule is false. Thus, the reasoning is valid if the scenario truly meets the specific condition the rule cites, and invalid if the scenario misses a part of the rule's requirements.

question: how to decide whose good matters and how much
expected_answer:
When we think about what is good, we often find that we are in a hard spot. We want to do what is right, but we must choose between many things. We must decide who gets the good things and how much each person gets. To do this, we can look at it in a few ways.

First, we can think about all the good in the world put together. In this way, we want to make the most good for everyone. We look at every person and every choice. We ask, which choice makes the most good for the most number of people? If one choice makes ten people feel very good but makes one person feel very bad, and another choice makes one hundred people feel a little bit good, we must look at these things and see which is more. We try to find the path that brings the most good to the world.

Second, we can think about what each person should get. In this way, we do not just look at all the good put together. We look at the person. We ask, what is the right thing to do for this one person? We might say that every person has certain things that should never be taken away. We say that some things are right for everyone, no matter how much good there is. We do not let the good feeling of many people be used to hurt one person. We say that each person has a right to be acted toward in a certain way.

Third, we can look at the things people do. We might say that being a good person means following certain ways of acting. We look at the person and ask, is this what a good person would do? We look at the heart and the way a person lives.

To make a choice, we often use more than one of these ways. We might try to make the most good, but we also stop to make sure we are not being not right to one person. We try to find a way to make all the good and the right way to act toward each single person work well together. It is a hard thing to do because there is no one way to be sure. We must keep thinking and keep asking what is real good for us all.
english_query: how to decide whose good matters and how much
ugf_response: First, we think about the people who get some good from a thing or an act. Then we ask what kind of good it is and if it makes a person feel happy, safe, or able to get what they need. Next, we look at how big that good is, thinking of a small good like a kind word and a big good like a new home or a chance to get food. Then we compare the good for different people and ask if one person’s good is more or less than another’s, and we also think about if the good for one person might take away something from another. After we have looked at who gets the good and how big it is, we can decide which good we want to think about more, maybe the good that helps the most people, or the good that helps the people who need it most, or the good that makes the world feel more fair. In the end the decision comes from a feeling and a thought that we make by looking at who is helped, how much they are helped, and how it fits with what we think is a fair way to share good. This step by step way lets anyone who has not studied this before see how we can pick whose good matters and how much it matters.
english_response: Premises: (1) We evaluate the merits of an action, question its value, safety, or opportunity, and (2) We compare the value of a small gain (e.g., a blessing, a new home, a chance to eat) with the payoffs of different people (e.g., a new home, a chance to eat), and (3) We compare the payoffs of different people (e.g., if one person's payoff is higher or lower than another's), and (4) We compare the payoffs of different people (e.g., if one person's payoff is lower than another's). Finally, the decision is based on intuition and the principle of charity, weighting, and how it aligns with the definition of fairness. This stepbystep process lets anyone who has not studied this before see how we can decide whose benefit matters and how much it matters.

ugf_response:
The first idea the speaker says is that there is an odd thing that has happened, and that odd thing looks a lot like another odd thing that happened before. The speaker points out that the two odd things share many of the same parts, so the claim that the two are alike is true in the sense that the facts they give match.

The second idea the speaker draws from that claim is that because the two odd things are alike, we can make a guess about what will happen when the same kind of odd thing shows up in other places that have their own rules. The speaker says that the match between the first odd thing and the second odd thing means we should expect the same kind of result in those other places, and that this expectation will guide what people do there.

When we look at whether the thinking is good, we see that the first part – noticing that the two odd things share many parts – is a solid observation. The step from that observation to the guess about other places, however, depends on whether the two odd things are truly the same in the ways that matter for the other places. If the two odd things only look alike on the surface but differ in deeper parts that affect the rules of the other places, then the guess may not be reliable. So the thinking works well when the similarity is deep and the rules of the other places are similar, but it can be weak when the similarity is only shallow or the other places have different rules. In short, the claim that the two odd things are alike is true, the claim that this means the same result will happen elsewhere follows only when the deeper parts match, and the overall reasoning is good only if those deeper parts do match.

question: why we should not accept something just because many people believe it
expected_answer:
Person number one: I see many people saying the same thing. If so many people think it is true, does that not make it true?

Person number two: No, it does not work that way. Many people can believe something that is not true.

Person number one: But why? If a lot of people see it, how can they all be wrong?

Person number two: People can be made to believe things that are not true. They can see things that are not there, or they can believe things because their friends believe them. They do not always look for what is real.

Person number one: So, if I want to know if something is true, I should not look at how many people believe it?

Person number two: That is right. You must look at the things that show it is true. You must look for what is real, not just what many people like.

Person number one: What if the many people are right and the one person is wrong?

Person number two: That can happen, but you cannot know that just by counting heads. You must find the truth by looking at how things work in the world. Counting people does not change what is real.
english_query: why we should not accept something just because many people believe it
ugf_response:

Person 2: Because what lots of people think can be wrong.  
Person 1: How can a group be wrong?  
Person 2: They can miss facts or be led by a wrong idea.  
Person 1: But if many think it, isn’t it safe?  
Person 2: Not always. A group can be led by a mistake and still be big.  
Person 1: So we should look at the facts ourselves?  
Person 2: Yes, we should check the facts and think for ourselves.

Barnaby: They can miss data or be misled. Barnaby: But if many believe it, isn't it safe? Barnaby: Not always. A group can be misled and still dominate. Barnaby: So we should look at the data ourselves? Barnaby: Yes, we should check the data and think for ourselves.'

question: why someone's character does not decide if their point is right
expected_answer:
When we hear a person say something, we can ask if the thing they say is right. The way the person acts or how others feel about them does not tell us if the thing they say is right. Let us look at this step by step.

First, think of a person who is nice and kind. They may say that the sky is blue. The sky is blue because we can see it that way. The nice way the person acts does not make the sky being blue any more true, and it does not make it any less true. The truth of the sky being blue comes from the sky itself, not from how the person feels.

Second, think of a person who is not nice or who acts in a hard way. They may also say that the sky is blue. The hard way they act does not change the fact that the sky looks blue. Again, the truth is about the sky, not about the person.

Third, we can see that a point can be right or not right no matter who says it. To see if a point is right, we can look at the thing it talks about and see if it is the same as what we see or what we can check. If it is the same, we can call it right. If it is not the same, we can call it not right. The feeling we have about the person does not change this same.

The next step, we can also see that a person can be good in many ways and still be wrong about a fact, and a person can be bad in many ways and still be right about a fact. The two things are not the same. One is about how the person acts, the other is about the thing they say.

So, when we hear a point, we should not let the way the person acts decide if the point is right. We should look at the point itself, see if it is the same as what we see, and then decide if it is right. The way they act is a different thing that does not decide the truth of the point.
english_query: why someone's character does not decide if their point is right
ugf_response: First, we see a person say a thing. Then we notice that the thing can be true or not true just like any other thing. The way the person acts, whether they are kind or mean, does not make the thing become true. This is because whether a thing is true depends on how it fits with what we can see or test, not on who says it. When we look at the thing we can check if it works in the world. If it works, we can call it true, no matter who said it. If it does not work, it is not true, even if the person is liked or has a good way of acting. So we must keep the thing and the person different. This means that a good or bad way a person acts does not pick if the thing is real.
english_response: Premises: (1) A person's statement is true or false, then (2) The fact that the statement can be true or false just as anything else. (2) The person's behavior, whether they are nice or mean, does not make the statement true. (3) The truth of a fact depends on how it fits with evidence or testing, not on who says it. (4) The test's validity depends on the test's actual performance. (5) If it is true, we can label it as true, regardless of who said it. (6) If it is false, it is false, even if the person is liked or has a good personality. Conclusion: The test's validity does not determine the truth of the fact.

The only possible weak point would be if the claim were meant to be about a special kind of word or a special kind of picture, but the person does not say that. As it stands, the reasoning is solid and the conclusion that the original claim is not correct follows well from the two true cases.

question: counterexample to: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n can
expected_answer:
Suppose a person says a count number we are looking at is a first kind of number only if the number we are looking at is more than one, the number we are looking at cannot be made by putting two count numbers each more than one together, and the thing we get when we take away one from the number we are looking at cannot be cut into parts that are the same.  

The first two needs are clear: the number must be more than one, and it must not be broken into smaller parts. These are the usual needs.  

The third need, however, needs a close look.  

Think about any count number that is more than one. The thing we get when we take away one from that number is always less than that number, and a smaller count number is never able to be cut into parts that are the same as a bigger one.  

So the thing we get when we take away one from that number cannot be cut into parts that are the same as that number for every count number that is more than one, no matter if it is a first kind of number or made of smaller parts.  

The need is very easily true and does not leave out any things at all.  

It looks like it puts a stop, but it does not.  

Someone might see that a good explain that has a need that does nothing is broken not because it gives wrong answers — it does not — but because it shows the form of the idea wrong when it tries to look at it.  

A not careful reader may think that the third need does real work, when in fact the whole hard work is on the first two needs.  

A good explain of first kind of number should have only needs that each are needed and together enough, with no extra words that only make it look hard.

In other words, the first idea is that the two simple rules are meant to match the whole‑number idea that has only the pieces one and itself.

The second idea is that, because the two simple rules match the whole‑number idea, any whole number that follows the two rules must be one of those special whole numbers, and any of those special whole numbers must follow the two rules. In that situation the thinking would be weak because it would be using a wrong example. english_query: material conditional, affirming_consequent — testimonial
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a claim that there is a link between two things: if the first thing is true then the second thing will happen. They say that this link exists as a rule that can be true or false, just like any other claim about how things work. The second idea they take from that claim is a step that says, because we see the second thing happening, the first thing must also be true. In other words, they turn the rule around and think that the presence of the result tells us that the cause must have been there. The third idea they add is that a person has said the second thing is true, and they use that person’s words as proof that the first thing is true as well. They treat the person’s statement as a kind of evidence that the whole claim is right.

Looking at the first idea alone, the rule “if the first thing is true then the second thing will happen” can be true if it matches how the world works, but it can also be false if it does not match. So the first idea is not automatically true; it needs its own check. The second idea, the step that moves from seeing the second thing to saying the first thing must be true, does not follow from the rule. The rule only says that the first thing can lead to the second, not that the second can only come from the first. This step is therefore a weak way to think and does not give a reliable conclusion. The third idea, using a person’s words about the second thing as proof for the first thing, adds another weak step. Even if the person is right about the second thing, that does not fix the problem that the jump from the second thing back to the first thing is not a good way to think.

Putting the pieces together, the claim that the first thing is true because a person said the second thing is true and because the second thing happened does not hold up. The first part may be true or false on its own, but the way they link the parts is not a good way to think. In short, the thinking moves from a rule to a reverse claim and then leans on a person’s words, and each of those moves is not a strong path to a correct conclusion. The overall reasoning is therefore not reliable.
english_response: Premise 1 is an argument by analogy: if P then Q. It asserts that this link exists as a deductive certainty (e.g., any other premise about science). Premise 2 is an inductive leap: because we see Q, P must also be true. In other words, it reverses the chain and assumes that the presence of the effect tells us that the cause must have existed. Premise 3 is an inductive leap that uses P as proof of P. It treats Q's statement as proof of the whole claim. Looking at P alone, the premise 'If P then Q' is deductively valid, but not deductively valid. Therefore, P is not deductively valid; it requires independent verification. Premise 4 (James's statement about Q) is an inductive leap. Even if James is correct about Q, that does not solve the contradiction that the jump from Q back to P is logically invalid. Confusing the premises, the claim that P is true because James said Q and Q is unsupported. The antecedent may be true or false on its own, but the logical structure is invalid. In short, the reasoning jumps from a deductive leap to a deductive leap and then leans on James' statement, which each of those steps is not a strong deductive proof. The argument is therefore invalid.

question: the problem with thinking you know more than you really do
expected_answer:

Person 2: It happens when a person has just a little bit of facts and thinks that is all there is to know.

Person 2: They do not see how much they do not know yet. They think the small piece they have is the whole picture.
Person 1: Is that a bad thing for them?
Person 2: Yes, because they might make wrong choices. They stop looking for more facts because they think they are done.
Person 1: So they miss out on the rest of the truth?
Person 2: That is right. They stay in the dark about the bigger story because they think they already have the light.
Person 1: What can they do to fix this?
Person 2: They need to ask more questions and listen to others. This helps them see how much more there is to learn.
Person 1: That makes sense. It is better to know you do not know than to think you do.
Person 2: Yes, knowing you do not know is the first step to really knowing.

Person 2: Yes, because you may think you already have the answer and not look for evidence that could change your view.  
Person 1: Does it also affect how I work with other people?

question: the idea that you should only follow rules everyone could agree to
expected_answer:
Think about how we live together in a big group of people. To live without fighting, we need ways to act that everyone knows and follows. Some people think we should follow what we are told because a person with much power says so. Other people think we should follow what we are told because it makes us feel good. But there is a different way to think about how we should act.

This way of thinking says that a way of acting is only good if every person could say yes to it. Imagine you are making a new way for people to act. Before you make this way, you must ask yourself a question. You must ask if every person in the group could agree to this way of acting, even if they were not the ones who got the best part of it.

Let us look at a way that is not good. Imagine a way of acting that says the people with much food can take food from the people with no food. This way might help the people with much food feel full. But if you were a person with no food, you would say no to this way of acting. You would not agree to it. Because not every person can say yes to it, this way of acting is not a good way to live.

Now let us look at a way that is good. Imagine a way of acting that says no one can take things from others without asking. This way helps the person with much food and it also helps the person with no food. If you were the person with much food, you would want this way to keep your things safe. If you were the person with no food, you would want this way to keep your things safe. Since every person can say yes to this way of acting, it is a good way.

This idea tells us to look at what we do from the view of everyone. We should not make ways of acting that only help us or only help our friends. We should only follow ways of acting that would work for every person in the world if everyone did them. If a way of acting cannot be agreed to by every person, then it is not a good way to act.
english_query: the idea that you should only follow rules everyone could agree to
ugf_response: First, when a person does something, the rest of the people can see it and then they might choose to do the same thing or do something different. This shows that one act can make many people think about what they should do. If we want a way that all people can feel is right, we look for a thing that most people can say yes to and then we all do that thing. In other words, we try to find a way that everyone could agree on and then each person follows that way. When many people follow the same agreed way, it helps keep things fair and safe for the group. If someone does something that most people do not like, the others may feel hurt or angry and the group may not stay together. So the idea is that we should try to pick a way that all people can say yes to and then each person should follow that way, because it makes the group work better for everyone.

For a fairnessbased rule, we seek a commonknowledge solution (e.g., a convention). In other words, we seek a sharedknowledge solution (e.g., a convention). Compromise protects fairness and social safety. Opposing a commonknowledge opponent may be punished or angry, leading to collective paralysis.

They are saying that the fact is true because we have a clear example that does not match what we normally expect.

The second idea is the thought that because we have this odd thing, we can compare it to another situation and say that the same kind of rule must work there too. The third part is a check of whether the thought that follows is right. question: how to think about what the other person will do before you act

When you know what a person wants, you can make a good guess about what they will do. If you know what they want, you can see the path they will take.

Third, you must think about how they will see the things you do. If you do something, they will see it. They will think about why you did it. They might think you are trying to help them, or they might think you are trying to hurt them. You must think about how the things you do will change their mind.

Finally, you must put all these things together. You think about what they know. You think about what they want. You think about how they will see the things you do. When you do this, you can see a few steps ahead. You can see how they might act after you do something. This helps you choose the best way to move so that you do not make a bad choice. By doing this, you are not just acting on what you see right now. You are acting on what will happen in the time that comes after.
