question: literal_similarity analogy (explanatory) — mechanical_and_repair
expected_answer:
The reason that taking out air from a thing with two wheels that stops the wheel water line makes the stick you push feel hard is the same reason that taking out air from a car part that makes the wheel turn fast water line makes the foot stick feel hard, and the way we show it shows that the two jobs are not just the same but work in the same way.  

In any water push system, force is moved by a water line that does not get smaller: push at one end of the line and the same push appears at the other end, with no drop and no wait.  

Air, however, can get smaller — push an air ball and it gets smaller instead of moving push — and an air ball anywhere in the water line means that the foot stick or the push stick first push the air ball before any push reaches the part that holds the wheel or the small moving part that works with the other part.  

What we see in both cases is a soft feel: the moving part moves more than we think before it starts to work and the feel is not straight.  

The fix in both cases is the same because the problem is the same: push new water that does not get smaller through the system with a strong push, carry the air that can get smaller out through an open small hole at the far end of the water line, and close the hole before letting the moving part push go so that the empty space behind the part that moves back can not pull air back in.  

The good look in both cases is the same: a hard same moving part from the first little bit of travel with no soft feeling.  

A person who knows why thing with two wheels that stops the wheel air taking out works not just the steps but the water push idea already has the whole story for the car part that makes the wheel turn fast.  

The size of the systems changes the some water changes and the number of hands needed changes but the ideas that make things stop working and the ideas that make the fix work are the same.

They point out that the shape and the look of the two parts match, so they claim that this match is a fact that can be trusted. The second idea they think follows from that fact is that because the two parts look the same, the way to fix them must also be the same. They use the picture of the two parts to explain why the same tool or the same steps that work on the machine part should work on the car part as well. The third part of the case is the judgment about whether this line of thought is good. The thinking is not very strong because just because two things look the same does not always mean they work the same or need the same fix. Different machines can have hidden parts or different inner ways that are not seen on the outside, so a method that works on one may not work on the other. question: how two things happening together does not mean one causes the other
expected_answer: First, we see that two things can happen at the same time. One way to show this is to look at the sun and a bird. The sun comes up and the bird makes a loud sound. These two things happen together every day. Then we might think that the bird makes the sun come up because it makes the sound right before the light appears. But this is not true. The sun comes up because of the way the world turns, not because of the bird. So, just because two things happen near each other in time does not mean one makes the other happen. Next, we look for a real reason why things happen. We need to see if changing one thing changes the other. If we stop the bird from making the sound, the sun still comes up. This shows that the sound did not cause the sun to rise. Finally, we understand that seeing two things together is only a start. We must look deeper to find the real cause. We should not jump to a guess about cause just because things are tied in our eyes. We need to check if one thing really pushes the other to happen.

If the other one still happens the same way, then the first one is not the reason. If the other one changes when we change the first one, then the first one may be the reason. So just because two things happen together does not mean one makes the other happen.

To determine if one actually causes the other, we would need to observe the effect of conditioning on one of them while holding all other variables constant. If B still correlates, A is not the cause. If B changes when conditioning on A, A may be the cause. Thus, correlation does not imply causation.

question: the difference between a strong reason and a weak reason
expected_answer:
When a person looks at why they do one thing instead of another, they may feel that one reason feels strong and another reason feels not strong. The strong reason feels like it will really push what happens in one way, while the not strong reason feels like it may not do much at all. The hard question is how we can tell when a reason is strong and when it is not strong. It is not just a feeling, it is a question that can make us think a lot about how we decide what to do.

One way people answer is to ask if the reason has many facts that line up with what we already see. When a reason comes with many facts that line up with other things we know, many people say that makes the reason strong. They think that the more facts we have, the more we can be sure the reason will work the way we think. Others say that a reason can be strong even if it does not have a lot of facts, if it fits well with the big picture of what we already see. For them, the fit with the whole set of things we know is more important than a count of facts.

Another group of people look at how the reason makes us feel safe or safe from hurt. If a reason seems to keep us from hurt, they call it strong. If a reason seems to lead us toward a bad place, they call it not strong. They may also think about what the reason will let us do later. A reason that lets us reach more good things later can be called strong, while a reason that stops us from getting good things can be called not strong.

Still another set of people think about how many other people have used the same reason and seen it work. They look at the number of times the reason has been used and the number of times it has helped. If many people say it helped them, they say the reason is strong. If only a few people say it helped, they say it is not strong. Each of these answers comes from a different way of looking at the world, and each can make sense in its own way. The hard question stays the same: what makes a reason strong or not strong, and why do we feel one way about it and another way about another? The answer may be together of facts, fit with what we know, feeling of being safe, and the stories we hear from others.
english_query: the difference between a strong reason and a weak reason
ugf_response:
When people talk about why they do something, they give a reason. One reason can feel solid and can make many people feel it is a good cause to act, while another reason can feel shaky and can leave people unsure whether to act. The scene we look at is a person who says they will do something because they have a reason, and other people hear that reason and have to decide if it is strong enough to make them do the same thing or something else.

The hard question that comes up is how we can tell if a reason is strong or weak. Some people say a strong reason is one that fits with what we can see and check, that lines up with what has happened before, and that would lead to good results for the people involved. Others say a strong reason is one that feels right inside, that matches the ideas we hold dear, or that makes us feel safe and calm. Still other people think a weak reason is one that comes from only a little bit of information, that is based on a feeling that can change quickly, or that does not show a clear link to the result we want.

Different answers appear because people look at the same reason in different ways. One group of people will look for proof that the reason matches real facts, so they call it strong when they can see a clear connection between the cause and the effect. Another group will look at how the reason fits with their own ideas about what is right or what feels fair, so they call it strong when it matches those ideas, even if they cannot point to clear proof. A third group may say a reason is weak when it seems to come only from a quick feeling or from a wish that something will happen, because they think a reason needs more solid proof before they will act on it. Each of these views makes sense to the people who hold them, and the mix of these views shows why it can be hard to agree on where the line between a strong reason and a weak reason should be.

The underlying question is how we distinguish between a strong and weak argument (e.g., a claim about a motive) and a weak one (e.g., a claim about a motive) and the difficulty of distinguishing between a strong and weak argument (e.g., a claim that aligns with evidence, aligns with a held belief), and a weak one (e.g., satisfies belief), while others interpret a weak argument as a weak one based on a strong intuition, or lacks a concrete link to the desired outcome. Extensive responses arise because of different evaluations of the same argument. One group will seek evidence that the argument is logically consistent with evidence, so they label it as strong when recognizing a direct link between the cause and effect. Another group will evaluate the argument's logical connection with their own values or expectations, so they label it as strong when it aligns with those values, even though they cannot point to concrete evidence. A third group may label it weak when it seems to come only from a sudden mood or expectation, because they expect a reason to be more robust before acting on it. Each of these statements is rational to the audience, and the combination of them explains why it is counterintuitive where the arrow between a strong and weak argument should be.

question: what makes a good reason for believing something
expected_answer:
Person 1: What makes a good why to think something is right?  
Person 2: A good why is when you see many things that are the same and that do not change when you look at them in many ways.  
Person 1: Can you give a time?  
Person 2: Sure. When you see a ball fall down every time you let go, and the ball always goes down, that is a good why to think that the ball will go down when you let go.  
Person 1: What about when we can not see something?  
Person 2: Then we look for other things that fit together with what we already know. If many people have seen the same thing and tell the same talk, that can be a good why too.  
Person 1: Is a good why only about what we see?  
Person 2: No. A good why can also come from things we can feel or hear. If you hear a sound that always comes before a light, you can think the sound means the light will come.  
Person 1: How do we know when a why is not good?  
Person 2: When the things do not fit together, or they change many times, or people see not same things, then the why is not right.  
Person 1: So we should look for many things that are the same and do not change?  
Person 2: Yes, that is a good way to see if a why is right.
english_query: what makes a good reason for believing something
ugf_response:

Person 2: A good reason is something that fits with what we already see and can be checked.  
Person 1: How do we know if it fits with what we see?  
Person 2: We can compare it with many times we have seen the same thing happen.  
Person 1: What if we cannot see it directly?  
Person 2: Then we look for other things that point to it, like other people who have checked it.  
Person 1: How do we know those other people are not wrong?  
Person 2: We see if many different people get the same result each time.  
Person 1: So a good reason is when many people see the same thing and it matches what we know?  
Person 2: Yes, that is a strong sign that it may be true.

question: how to find the hidden parts of someone's case
expected_answer:
When you want to know the true story of what happened in a fight or a bad thing, you must look for the parts that are not seen. People often do not tell the whole story. They may hide things because they are afraid or because they want to look good. To find these hidden parts, you must act like a person who looks for things that are lost.

First, you must look at what people say. Listen to every person who was there. Do not just listen to the words they say, but also look at how they say them. If a person stops talking suddenly when you ask about a certain time or a certain thing, that is a sign. That silence is a way of hiding something. You should ask the same thing many times in different ways to see if their story stays the same. If the story changes, you have found a place where something is hidden.

Second, you must look at the things that were left behind. These are the things that do not lie. If a person says they were sleeping, but their shoes are wet and have the black wet earth from the ground on them, then the shoes are telling a different story. The shoes show what really happened. You must look at all the things that can tell you about the time and the place.

Third, you must look for what is not there. This is the hardest part. If a person tells a story about a big fight, but they do not say who started it, you must ask why that part is gone. If a story is missing a piece that should be there, that missing piece is where the truth is hiding.

Finally, you must put all these pieces together. You take what people said, what the things showed, and what was missing. You look for the points where they meet. When the things that were seen and the things that were hidden fit together into one big picture, you have found the truth of the matter.
english_query: how to find the hidden parts of someone's case
ugf_response: First, we look at what the person says and does in the open. Then we ask them to tell more about why they did each thing and what they felt inside. Next, we watch how they act when no one is looking so we can see the parts that are not shown at first. After that we ask other people who know the person to tell us what they have seen and heard. We also look at any paper or note the person has left that might give signs. By putting together what we see, what they tell us, what others tell us, and what the paper says, we can bring out the parts of the person's story that were not clear before. In this way we find the parts that were not in plain view.
english_response: Hint: Look at Mira's testimony and testimony in the open. Then ask them to explain each step and their internal sensations. Then ask other witnesses to reveal the hidden steps. Then ask other witnesses to tell us what they have seen and heard. By combining what we see, what they tell, what others tell, and what the report says, we can uncover the hidden parts of Mira's story. In this way, we uncover the hidden steps.

question: abstraction analogy (explanatory) — physics
expected_answer:
One of the most strange facts about a part of all we can see is that it has a lot of one kind of things and almost no different kind of things, even though the things that tell how things work say the two kinds should be the same in most ways.  

A person who learns and sees this not the same for the first time might ask: if the things that tell how things work are the same, why is the whole all not the same?  

The step where the same becomes not the same look, taken from ideas about things that pull, about small pulls that come together, and about long small things that turn, gives an easy answer.  

The look comes from three simple things that all have the same shape: the things that tell how things work are the same when you change something; a thing we can change – heat, pull, weight – goes past an important point; the thing then has two low ways it could be, both the same in how they work; and the thing by itself chooses one of those ways, ending up in a look that is less the same than the things that tell how it works.  

The main thought the look puts is that a thing that is the same can make something not the same even if nothing not the same was put in a way that is not seen at the start or in how it moves: the not the same comes from the choice itself, not from breaking the deep same.  

When we use this thought for one kind of things and the different kind of things, it works like this. The main things that people who learn use act like the thing that is the same. The getting colder of the early all we can see, as it changes from one step to another, acts like the thing we can change that goes past an important point. The two possible all that could be, one with more one kind of things and one with more the different kind of things, act like the two low ways. And the real all we live in, which has more one kind of things, is the thing that the thing chose by itself – it is less the same than the things that made it, but it still fits with the things being the same at the start.  

This way of looking at it quickly removes the looks like problem between a thing that is the same and a thing that is not the same: there is no problem, only a step where the same becomes not the same, which we know can happen.  

It also makes the still question clear: not why the all we see is not the same even though the things are the same, but what certain way made enough small change to move the choice to the big many of one kind of things we see.

question: how the way choices are shown can change what you pick
expected_answer: First, we think about how a person must pick one thing from many things. We want to know if the way we show these things changes what the person picks. Second, we think about how we can put these things in a line. If we put one thing first, it might look better than it is. This happens because the first thing the eyes see stays in the mind for a long time. Third, we think about how we can group things together. If we put two things that are like each other close, a person might see them as a single group. This can make the thing after the third thing look very different from the first two. Next, we think about how we can show how much of something there is. If we make one thing look very big, a person might think it is more important than the small things. The thing after that is how we can see that the way we show things can make a person feel a certain way. If we show only the good parts of a thing, the person will want it. If we show the bad parts, the person will not want it. Finally, we see that the way things are shown can change the choice made by a person even if the things themselves are the same.

ugf_response: First, we see that when many things to pick are put before us, we first look at the ones that stand out or that we see first. Then we see that those first things get more of our look, so we may maybe pick one of them. Next, we think that if the things are put in a place, the ones at the first part of the place feel easier to reach, so we may pick one of those. This means that the way the things are shown can push us toward a certain pick. Then we see that if the things are shown with pictures or with words that make them look good, we may feel that they are better and we pick them. This shows that the form of the show can change what we end up picking. Finally, we understand that by changing the look of the show we can change many people picks, even if the things themselves do not change.

(2) We assume that if the options are placed in a set, the firstchoice candidates feel easier to reach, so we may choose one of them. (3) We observe that if the options are presented with visual or emotive visual cues, we may feel better and choose them. (4) We understand that framing effects affect collective choices, even though the options themselves are unchanged.

question: why saying something over and over does not make it true
expected_answer:

Person two: No. The sky stays the color it is. Saying it many times only changes what is in your mind, not what is in the world.

question: why sometimes a good enough answer is better than the best answer
expected_answer:
Imagine you are building a house. You want the house to be the most perfect house in the whole world. You want the walls to be very strong and the floor to be very even and flat. You want every part of the house to be the very best it can be. But to make the house the very best, you must spend all your money and all your time. You might spend ten years making the windows perfect. While you are doing this, you have no place to live. You are cold and you want food because you are only working on the house.

The hard question is this. Is it better to have a house that is good enough to live in right now, or is it better to wait a long time to have a house that is the best?

Some people will say you must wait for the best. They think that if something is not the best, it is not good to do. They believe that if you stop before you reach the top, you have failed. They want everything to be perfect. They think that if you settle for something that is just good, you are being someone who does not want to work or someone who is not strong. They want the highest point of everything.

Other people will say that the good house is better. They say that time and money are things you cannot get back. If you spend all your time trying to make one thing perfect, you cannot do anything else. You might need to build a second house, or you might need to buy food, or you might need to help a friend. They think that being able to finish something and move to the next thing is more important than making one thing the very best. They think that a good answer that works right now can do more for you than a perfect answer that comes too late.

This is a fight between wanting things to be perfect and needing to live in the real world. The world moves fast. If you wait too long to find the best way to do something, the thing you are trying to do might not be needed anymore. The person who wants the best might end up with nothing, while the person who wants good enough might end up with many good things.
english_query: why sometimes a good enough answer is better than the best answer
ugf_response: When we look at a problem we can try to find a perfect answer that fixes everything the way we want. That perfect answer can be hard to find, can need a lot of time, and can need a lot of work that may not be ready when we need to act. At the same time we can find an answer that is not perfect but that works well enough for the moment. It may not solve every tiny part of the problem, but it can be ready quickly and can let us move forward. The hard question in this situation is whether we should wait for the perfect answer or use the good enough answer that we already have. Some people say that we should always wait for the perfect answer because any mistake can grow later and cost more. They think that a perfect answer gives us the safest path and that a small mistake now can become a big problem later. Others say that waiting for perfect can cost us time, money, or even the chance to help people now. They argue that a good enough answer lets us act, learn, and change the answer later if we need to. Some people also point out that in a fast changing world the perfect answer may not stay perfect for long, so using a good enough answer and then fixing it later can be the smarter way. Still other people say that the choice depends on how important the problem is. If the problem could hurt many people, they may feel that we must wait for the best answer. If the problem is small or the risk is low, they may feel that a good enough answer is the right choice. In the end the situation makes us think about the trade off between speed and safety, between using what we have now and waiting for what might be better later, and about how we judge when a good enough answer is truly enough.
english_response: In a fully satisficing problem, a perfect solution can be rare, costly, and timeconsuming when needed. Simultaneously, a satisficing solution may not solve every subset of the problem, but it can be quickly ready and let us move forward. The critical question in this scenario is whether we should wait for the perfect solution or adopt the satisficing heuristic. Some argue that we should always wait for the perfect solution because any mistake can grow later and cost more, assuming that a perfect solution gives us the safest path and that a small mistake now becomes a serious problem later. Others argue that waiting for perfect can cost us time, money, or even the chance to help people now. They argue that satisficing lets us act, learn, and adjust the future if needed. Nonetheless, others argue that the decision depends on the urgency of the problem. If the problem could harm many people, they may feel compelled to wait for the best solution. If the problem is small or the risk is low, they may feel satisficing is the rational choice. In the end, the scenario forces us to consider the tradeoff between speed and safety, between relying on what we have now and waiting for what might be better, and how we judge when satisficing is truly sufficient.

question: how we build a picture of the world from small pieces of what we see

question: analogy_proper analogy (explanatory) — cooking_and_craft
expected_answer:
When a person who makes food from a ground thing that grows and water talks about letting the together stuff rise, they are really talking about making a place where a small life thing can do its work and keep that work. The small life thing is already there; the person job is to give it the heat and water in the air it needs to break down food, and to trust the part that is not hard to hold the air the small life thing makes. Put the together stuff in a very cold place and the work moves very slowly. Put it in a very hot place and you kill the small life thing that does the work. Do it right and the whole together stuff gets bigger, you can see it, in about one hour.  

That whole thing – the person, the place, the heat and water in the air, the growing – looks a lot like what a good person who helps does when a group of people talk together to make new thoughts. The people are the small life things: they already have the thoughts you want. Your work is not to make the thoughts yourself but to keep the heat and water in the air right so that people feel safe enough to show them, and to give a way of talking that holds and joins what is said instead of letting it go away.  

If there is too much force in the room, people stop talking; if there is too little way to hold the talk, thoughts fly by and are not caught. Make the feeling in the room right and the place for thoughts gets bigger that you can see, in a way that feels like a real thing.  

This idea is good because it moves the look from what the thoughts are to the heat and water in the air that let them appear, and that is where the person who helps really has power. It also gives a good sign: even a perfect rise can be broken if you guess the wrong time to stop.  

If a talk is kept open too long, past the point where the thoughts grow well, it starts to fall down under its own weight like the food that has risen too much and then loses its shape.

Thus, the argument is useful, but it requires observing the inconsistency.

question: how to pick the best reason out of many that could be right
expected_answer:
Sometimes you have many different ways to tell why something happened or why something is true. You must find the one way that has the most chance of being the right one. To do this, you should look at each way one by one.

First, look at how much each way needs to be true. If a way to explain something needs many things to happen at the same time, it is probably not the best way. A good way to explain something should not need too many extra things to be true. If you have to believe ten things that you cannot see just to make your way work, then your way is not as good as a way that only needs one thing to be true.

Second, look at how much new stuff your way brings into the world. If you have two ways to explain why the ground is wet, and one way says it rained, while the other way says a big water machine fell from the sky and broke, the first way is better. The second way brings in a big machine that no one saw and no one can show. We should pick the way that does not add too many new things that we cannot see or touch.

Third, look at how well the way fits with what we already know. If a new way to explain something goes against everything we have seen before, we should be very careful. We should only pick that way if it is much, much better than all the other ways.

To find the best way, you take all these things and put them together. You want the way that needs the fewest things to be true, the way that does not add too many new things, and the way that stays close to what we already know is true. When you do this, you find the way that has the most chance of being the truth.
english_query: how to pick the best reason out of many that could be right
ugf_response: First, we see that there are many possible choices for why something happened. Then we look at the facts we have about the thing that happened. Next, for each possible choice we ask how well it matches those facts. If a choice matches most of the facts, we think it is a good match; if it matches only a few, we think it is a weaker match. After we have looked at all the matches, we compare them and see which one matches the most facts. We also think about how simple the choice is, because a simple match that fits the facts is often better than a very complicated one. Finally, we pick the choice that matches the most facts and is simple enough, and we call that the best reason.
english_response: Premises: (1) There are multiple explanations for why something happened, (2) Given the information we have about the event, and (3) Given each possible explanation, evaluate the test's accuracy. (2) If a solution is backed by the majority of the data, it is a good match; if it is backed by only a few, it is a weaker one. (3) Given the full set of matches, compare them and see which one supports the most evidence. (4) The simplicity of the test is a matter of convenience, because a simple match that fits the data is often better than a complex one. (5) The best explanation is to choose the solution that is simple enough, labeled as the best explanation.

question: when helping the group costs you but helps everyone
expected_answer:
Person number one: I have a question about what to do when doing something good for the whole group makes my own life harder.

Person number two: I can tell you about that. It is when you give up something you have so that all the people with you can have more or be better off.

Person number one: If I give up my own things, does it make me feel bad?

Person number two: It can. You might feel like you are losing. You might feel that things are not the same for everyone and that it is not right that you must lose while others do not.

Person number one: But if everyone does this, does the whole group become better?

Person number two: Yes. When each person gives a little bit of what they have, the group becomes very strong. The things that the group has can help everyone stay safe and happy.

Person number one: Is there a way to know if it is good to do?

Person number two: You look at what you lose and what the group gets. If the good that comes to the whole group is much bigger than the bad that comes to you, then it is a good thing to do.

Person number one: What if I am the only one doing it?

Person number two: Then it is very hard. If only one person gives and the others only take, that person will soon have nothing left. For it to work well, many people must do it together.
english_query: when helping the group costs you but helps everyone
ugf_response:

Person 2: Yes, it takes my free time and I feel tired, but I think it is right.  
Person 1: How does it help the rest of the group?  
Person 2: When the work is done, the other people can move on with their tasks and do not have to wait.  
Person 1: So your giving up time makes the whole group work better?

question: counterexample to: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object
expected_answer:
If a person looks at many pictures that clearly show what they are about — a picture of a rich man, a picture of a place with land and water, a picture of food that grows on a living thing — that person may think that a picture needs to look like the thing, that a person who looks can see the thing, and that the person who paints meant to show the thing. In each case, the shapes on the paper are the same as the parts of the thing that can be seen, a person who looks can see the thing easy, and the person who paints tried to show it. The big idea that this is the clear idea for what a picture is comes easy: these three parts hold what the cases have the same.

But the big idea is too small when we try to use it on other pictures. Imagine a person sees a picture of a sound thing with thin lines that make sound that is made in a kind that breaks the thing into many flat shapes. The paper shows many flat shapes that cover each other and show many views at the same time, and it does not look like a sound thing in the simple way we usually see a sound thing. A person who looks can not see the usual shape, the dark color of a tree, or the thin lines that make sound just by looking. The first part of the big idea is not met. Still, a person who looks who knows the usual way of this kind can see a sound thing in the picture. They know that the broken look is a way to show a thing, not a problem that stops the picture from showing it. The person who paints also meant to show a sound thing. The second and third parts are met. So the picture can be a picture of a sound thing in a good way. This makes us see that the idea we made from pictures that look like real things was wrong to think that looking like the thing is always needed. A picture can show a thing by using a usual way, by making a shape, and by learned power, even when it does not look like the thing in the way the idea says.
english_query: counterexample to: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object
ugf_response: The first idea says that a painting shows a thing when the picture looks like that thing. The second idea adds that the picture must also look like the thing whenever the painting shows that thing, so the two are said to go together in both directions. The third idea is a case that shows the first idea does not work in both directions: a painting can be made to tell us about a thing even though the picture does not look like the thing, for example a map that uses symbols to stand for a place or a portrait that uses very abstract shapes to stand for a person. This case shows that the claim that a painting shows a thing only when it looks like that thing is not always right. The thinking that follows from the first two ideas is therefore not good, because it takes the first idea as a rule that works for all paintings, but the case shows a clear break in that rule. So the overall argument that the rule works both ways is weak, and the case they give is a good way to point out the problem.
english_response: The premise establishes that a painting exhibits a property when the visual is a visual. The premise adds that the visual must also exhibit a property whenever the painting exhibits that property, so the two are explicitly stated to be correlated. The premise is a counterexample: a painting can be reevidential (e.g., a symbolic map or a abstract portrait). This example shows that the claim that a painting exhibits a property only when it is a visual is not necessarily true. The reasoning from the premises is therefore invalid, because it treats the premise as a universal rule for all artworks, but the example shows a direct violation of that rule. Thus, the overall argument that the rule is biconditional is weak, and the example they provide is a valid example of the contradiction.

question: anomaly analogy (predictive) — geometric
expected_answer: For about many many years, people who work with numbers tried to get the last thought from the first four thoughts, thinking that the idea that there is only one line that never meets a given line when you start from a point not on it was so clear that it did not need its own thought. The thinking felt strong: in any drawing you could make, only one line from the point seemed to go next to the given line without ever meeting it. The idea inside this thinking was that any drawing of space that follows the first four thoughts would also have to follow the last thought. People checked this idea in the 1800 years and found it wrong when they built a kind of space that is not flat. In that not flat space, the first four thoughts work just as the old one said. Lines go on always, all right corners are the same, and you can draw circles. But from any point not on a line, there are many many lines that never meet the first line. The space between the two lines that come near the first line makes a small space, and any line inside that space will never meet the first line. The old idea that there is only one such line is not just a little wrong, it is very wrong: where the flat space has only one line that never meets, the not flat space has many many lines that never meet. The thing that comes from the fact that the not flat space is the other way, which makes lines move away from each other faster than flat space would make them. That other way of being not flat is not part of the first four thoughts; it is part of the last thought, and if it is there or not decides how all space looks. The look between flat space and not flat space works for the first four thoughts but stops at the last thought, and this stop is not a small wrong but a clear line that makes two different sets of thoughts far.

The second idea that follows from that claim is that the odd thing looks a lot like an odd thing that has been seen in a different picture. Because the two odd things look alike, the person says we can compare them and then guess that the rule that made the first odd thing happen will also make the second odd thing happen. In other words, they think the first odd thing can be used as a guide to guess what will happen with the second.

The third idea is an evaluation of the thinking. The line of thought works if the two odd things are truly made by the same hidden rule, but it can be weak if the two pictures are only similar on the outside. The reasoning is okay when there is other proof that the same hidden rule is at work, but it is not very strong when the only link is that the two odd things look alike. So the thinking can be useful as a first guess, but it should not be taken as a firm proof without more checking.

Specifically, the claim that the 'straw man' is present and observable is true. The conclusion follows necessarily from the premise that the 'straw man' appears strongly to be a 'straw man'. Since the 'straw man' is identical, the speaker suggests a comparison and inferring that the rule that caused the 'straw man' will also cause the 'straw man'. Specifically, they assume the 'straw man' is used as a guide to predict the 'straw man'. The conclusion is logically valid if the 'straw man' is actually caused by the same hidden rule, but weak if the 'straw man' is superficially similar. The reasoning is valid if there is independent evidence that the hidden rule is active, but weak if the only link is the 'straw man'. Thus, the reasoning can be useful as a premise, but it should not be treated as definitive proof without further testing.

A big sky change is coming, and the question is not if we change plans but how fast. I have been thinking about sleeping in a small soft house in the back grass with my child night. That plan is stopped. Any thing that is coming moves fast enough to arrive by night, and being in a small soft house when a big air change comes is not a choice I would do again.

question: counterexample to: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point
expected_answer:
Think about three things that always go without big changes.  

The first thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives the number times itself. It goes without big changes at every point on the line of true numbers. It works everywhere; for every line of numbers that gets closer to a given point, the new line of numbers also gets closer to the number times itself of that point; and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that also stay not too big.  

The second thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives a number that gets bigger fast when the number that goes in gets bigger. It goes without big changes at every point. It works everywhere; it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to a point, the new line also gets closer; and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that also stay not too big.  

The third thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives a number that goes up and down in the same way. It goes without big changes at every point. It works everywhere; it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to a point, the new line also gets closer; and it itself stays not too big, so it also turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big.  

From these cases we can make a thought: a thing that changes a number into another number is without big changes at a point if and only if it works there, it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to that point the new line also gets closer, and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big in every small area around the point.  

The first two thoughts are the well known way of saying without big changes with lines of numbers, which is the same as the usual small number thought and everyone says it is right.  

The third thought – that the thing turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big in small areas around the point – is true for all three case things and might look like a different needed thought. Yet it is not different. It comes from the line thought. If a thing keeps the idea that every line of numbers that gets closer to a point also gets closer after the thing, then it stays not too big in every small area around that point, and so it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big in that area into sets of numbers that stay not too big. The third thought adds no new thought that is already in the second. The case things were all good things that showed many nice things beyond just being without big changes, and the thought has taken one such nice thing – staying not too big – and raised it up as a different thought, when in truth it follows from the line thought as a clear end. The look at this has taken true words about without big changes things for a different thought, making the thought look more strong without adding any real new thought.

question: why some deals hold together and others fall apart
expected_answer:
When two or more people agree to do something together, they make a promise. Sometimes these promises stay strong for a long time. Other times, the promises break and the people stop working together. To know why this happens, we must look at how the people think and how they act.

First, we must look at what each person gets from the promise. If one person gets much more than the other, the person with less may feel that the promise is not good. They might think that it would be better to walk away and do something else. If the things they get are not enough to make them happy, the promise will probably break.

Second, we must look at what happens if someone breaks the promise. If a person can break the promise and get more than they had before, they will probably do it. This is true if they can do it without being made to pay for it. If there is no way to make them pay for breaking their word, they have no reason to keep it. But if they know that breaking the promise will make them lose something important later, they will try to keep it.

Third, we must look at how much the people know. Sometimes, one person knows much more about the thing they are doing than the other person. This can be a problem. The person who knows less might be made to believe something that is not true. If they find out they were made to believe something that is not true, they will stop working with the first person. For a promise to stay strong, both people need to feel that they are seeing the same thing and that no one is hiding something.

The thing after the third thing we must look at is the people themselves. If the people do not trust each other, the promise will be hard to keep. Trust is the feeling that the other person will do what they said they would do. If you think the other person might hurt you or take your things, you will not want to make a promise. Even if the promise looks good on paper, not having trust can make it fall to pieces.

Finally, we must look at what happens in the future. If the people can work together many times, they will try to be good to each other. They know that if they are bad now, they will not have more promises later. But if they will only work together one time, they might try to take as much as they can right now. This is because they do not care about what happens after the first time.

So, a promise stays together when both sides get what they need, when it is hard to break the promise without losing something, when everyone knows the truth, when there is trust, and when the people want to work together many times. If these things are not there, the promise will probably break.
english_query: why some deals hold together and others fall apart
ugf_response: First, when two or more people say they will do something together they make a promise. Then each person must keep what they said and must feel that the other people also keep theirs. If everyone keeps the promise the promise stays together and the people can keep working. If one person does not keep what they said the other people may stop keeping theirs too and the promise can break. The chance that a promise stays together gets stronger when the people talk a lot, when they understand what each other needs, when they feel safe that the other will not hurt them, and when the promise fits what each can do. When the promise is too hard, when people do not talk, when one person changes what they can do, or when someone feels they are not being treated fair, the promise can fall apart. So trust, clear talk, fair treatment and the ability to do what was promised keep a promise together, while broken trust, no talk, feeling hurt and too much change make a promise fall apart.
english_response: Premises: (1) When two or more agents commit to a joint venture, each must keep their word and feel that the others also keep their word. (2) If everyone keeps the word, the commitment is selfreinforcing and cooperation can be sustained. (3) If one defects, the others may defect too, and the commitment can collapse. (4) The probability of a commitment becomes stronger when players are loud, communicate, anticipate each other's needs, are safe, and aligned with the rules. (5) Overreacting, defection, deviating, or being treated unfairly, the commitment can collapse. (6) Therefore, trust, explicit communication, fair treatment, and the ability to meet a precommitment sustain cooperation, while sabotage, silence, morale, and overreacting undermine the commitment.

question: how the way choices are shown can change what you pick
expected_answer: First, we look at how a person must pick between things. We think that a person will pick what is best for them. But, the way we show the things to pick can change what that person picks. We see this when we give a person a group of things to choose from. If we show many things, the person might pick something different than if we show only two things. This happens because our minds look at how things are the same or how they are not the same. To show this, if we show a thing that is very bad and a thing that is a little bad, the person might pick the little bad thing and think it is good. But if we show that same little bad thing next to a thing that is very good, the person might pick the very good thing instead. This means the way we put the things together changes how the person sees how much a thing is good for them. Second, we see that the order of the things can change the pick. If we show a very good thing first, the person might feel like they have already won and pick something less good later. Third, we see that how we say things about the choices can change the pick. If we say a thing will help you, you might want it more. If we say a thing will not hurt you, you might feel the same way. Even if the two ways of saying it mean the very same thing, the way we say it makes the person feel different. In the end, we see that the way we show choices can lead a person to pick something they might not pick if the things were shown in a different way.
english_query: how the way choices are shown can change what you pick
ugf_response: First, we notice that when we are asked to pick something we often see a few things at a time. Then we see that the order of those things can make us look at the first one more. This means that the first thing gets more attention because our eyes go there first. Next, we think that if the first thing looks easy or nice we may think it is the best. This can push us to pick it even if another thing might be better. Then we see that the way the things are put together can make us think they belong together, so we may pick a thing from the same group as the first. This means the way the choices are shown can change what we end up picking.
english_response: Recognizing that the selective nature of choices leads to a stronger evaluation of the first candidate, revealing that the first candidate is more likely to be praised because of prior attention. By assuming that if the first candidate looks 'simple' or 'simple', we may rationalize the choice even if another candidate might be better, and by recognizing the framing effect, the framing effect changes the outcome.

question: counterexample to: An inference from particular instances to a general conclusion is inductively st

question: the problem of hurting one person to help many
expected_answer:
Imagine a group of people who are all in a bad way. Many people are about to die or be very hurt. Now, imagine there is one person who is not in a state where they might be hurt. If we do something to hurt this one person, we can stop the bad thing from happening to the many people. This is a hard thing to think about.

First, we must look at the side that wants to help the many. This side says that the best way to act is to make sure the most people are happy or safe. If one person gets hurt but ten people are saved, this side says that is the right thing to do. They look at how much good there is and how much bad there is. They want the most good for the most people.

Second, we must look at the side that wants to keep the one safe. This side says that every person is important. It is not okay to hurt a person just to help others. They say that if we start hurting people to save others, then no one is ever really safe. They believe that doing something bad to a person is wrong, even if it leads to something good later.

This is a fight between two ways of thinking about what is right. One way looks at the big group and wants the most good for everyone in that group. The other way looks at the single person and says that we must not do bad things to them. There is no easy answer to this. If we follow the first way, we might do things that feel very bad and wrong. If we follow the second way, we might let many people die when we could have stopped it. This is a problem that people still think about today.
english_query: the problem of hurting one person to help many

This makes us think about what is the right thing to do. We can look at two parts: the part where one person feels hurt, and the part where many people get good. We can think that the hurt to one is a bad thing, but the good to many is a good thing. When we put these two parts together, we have to decide if the good to many is big enough to make the bad to one okay. Some people say that we should not let anyone feel hurt, even if many get good, because the hurt is not right. Others say that if the good is very big, it may be okay to let the one feel hurt. This makes the choice hard. In the end, we see that the idea is about looking at a bad thing that hits one against a good thing that hits many, and there is no easy answer that works for every case.

Constructing these two inequalities requires deciding whether the value of the benefit to many is sufficiently high to justify the harm to one. Some argue that we should not let anyone suffer, even if many get something good, because the harm is unfair. Others claim that if the benefit to many is huge, it may be acceptable to let the one suffer, making the decision difficult. In the end, we see that the principle hinges on comparing pain vs. wellbeing, and there is no equilibria.

question: indicative conditional, paradoxical — perceptual

The first idea says a very normal and strong way about seeing what you know: if a seeing feeling is what you know about a thing, then there must be no possible other case that looks the same from the inside, where a person has the same feeling but the thing is not there.  

The way says a feeling that you are certain you know something by seeing, your place of knowing must be different from a place where you only seem to see it while being made to think wrong.  

What you know, in this way, needs a kind of safe that stops the other case that looks the same.  

The second idea is a note about the possible way of people with a body that see: for any seeing feeling that a normal person has, there is a possible other case—a very clear not real feeling—that looks the same from the inside and where the thing is not there.  

This is not a strange idea; head moves and some people show that the eye system can make feelings of any form and size without the thing being there.  

The thinking set of a bad thing or a head in a box is just a way to put this real possible thing together.  

From the two ideas, the final idea comes out as true: no seeing feeling that a normal person with a body has is what you know about a thing.  

The final idea is not strange in the clear way; it comes out without any wrong step.  

But it is strange in the way we think about knowing, because it goes against the strong belief that seeing is the first thing of what we know about the outside world.  

The point of the talk is not to accept the final idea but to push it on the first idea.  

People who talk about safe ways say that the important other cases are not all clear possible cases but only those that are close to the ways of the world—and very clear not real feelings are far from the ways of the world.  

People who say real feeling and not real feeling are not the same, so the second idea says the same feeling from the inside does not make sense.  

Each answer is a true thinking help, and the strange thing is the strong thing that moves them.

A is correct and consistent with empirical data. The jump to a major change in perception is poorly supported, making the argument weak. question: literal_similarity analogy (explanatory) — analytical
expected_answer:
the idea that the usual way something acts over a space can be found at a special inside point shows up two times in the first part of the study of change, one time for the fast change idea and one time for the adding up idea, and the two things that happen use the same kind of way we tell why even though they start with different ideas and different ways we show them

both say that the usual how something acts over a space is found by the thing at some inside point

the fast change idea says that if the thing can be changed without jumps then the usual how fast it changes over a space – the how high the line that joins the two ends goes – is found as the how fast it changes at one time at some inside point

the usual how high is found; there is a time when the how fast it changes is the same as the usual how fast it changes

the adding up idea says that if the thing has no jumps then the usual how much it is over a space – the all added together and then shared by the long of the space – is found as the thing has its own number at some inside point

the usual how much is found; there is a place where the thing has a number that is the same as its own usual how much

both ideas tell why the usual how something acts has a true place: not just that the usual how high is a number, but that the thing really has that how high somewhere; not just that the usual how much is a number, but that the thing really has that number somewhere

the ways we show them are different: the fast change idea can be turned into the idea that if a thing starts and ends at the same high then there is an inside place where the change is nothing, by taking away the line that joins the two ends and looking for a most big or most small inside place; the adding up idea uses the idea that if a thing goes from a low number to a high number it must pass every number in between, used on a thing with no jumps on a space that has both ends

both ways finally rest on the idea that the number line has no parts that are not there, which makes sure that many numbers that only go up or only go down will get close, that most big and most small places of a thing with no jumps on a space with both ends are there, and that a thing with no jumps will take every number in between

the fast change idea and the adding up idea are two ways to say the same idea – the usual how something acts has an inside place – used in two different number work places

When we look at the thinking, we see that the step from the first idea to the second idea depends on more than just the matching part. Just because two things share a look does not always mean that the reasons behind one are the same as the reasons behind the other. The thinking would be stronger if there were more proof that the inside parts or the causes of the two things are also the same, not only the outside look. Just because two things share a pattern does not automatically mean the causes of one are the causes of the other. question: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if
expected_answer:
One may say that real past change is made of more good things for people, more ways to act for people, and more ways to make and share ideas. This way of talking shows the money, the way people are told what to do, and the thinking parts that we most often join with the idea of change.  

But a story that shows this not enough can be taken from the past of big machine life.  

Imagine a group of people, over a long time of many years, see a big rise in the good life for most of them: food is more, homes are safer, and sick is less. At the same time, the number of ways to act grows: the chance to choose who leads is given to more, things that stop what people say are less, and laws that keep people safe are stronger. And the ways of the group to make and share ideas get very big because of new schools, the making of pages that are made by pressing letters, and the start of rooms with books that anyone can use. All three parts of the idea are true.  

But we should not say this change is all good, because at the same time the group makes big machine ways that hurt the earth in ways that may not be able to be fixed. The good things in life, ways to act, and ideas come at the thing we pay for hurting the earth that puts at the chance of the staying of those good things.  

It looks like the idea, by only asking for more good life, more ways to act, and more ideas, does not ask that these more things can stay and not be taken away from the children that come later. If we do not have that ask, a group may meet every part of change while slowly breaking the place of its own staying good.
english_query: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if
ugf_response:
The first point they say is true is that a group of people has gotten better in the past during a time when a certain thing is true about that group and that time. They point to the past and say that when the thing is there, the group moves forward and shows signs of getting better.  

From that they think a second point follows: that the certain thing must always be there for any group to get better, and that if a group does not have that thing, then it cannot have gotten better. question: why what you already believe changes how you see new things
expected_answer:
You have a set of thoughts in your head about how the world works. These thoughts are like a way of seeing things. When you see something new, you do not see it in a way that is clean and free from what you already know. Instead, you look at the new thing through the way you already think.

First, think about how you see colors. If you think the sky is always blue, you will look at the sky and look for blue. You will not look for other colors because your mind is already set on blue. This is how your thoughts work with new things too.

Second, your mind wants to feel that it is right. It is not a good feeling to think that what you know is wrong. When you see something new that goes against what you believe, your mind tries to change the new thing so it fits with your old thoughts. You might tell yourself that the new thing is not true or that it is not important. This makes you feel safe in your own mind.

Third, your mind looks for things that say you are right. If you believe that all dogs are mean, and you see a new dog, you will look for the mean things in that dog. You might see it make a loud and angry sound and say, I knew it. But if you see the dog being kind, you might say it is only acting that way to make you think something that is not true. You find the things that are the same as your old thoughts and you throw away the things that are not the same.

Because of this, two people can look at the very same thing and see two different things. One person sees a thing that proves they are right, and the other person sees a thing that proves they are right. They are not looking at the thing in a way that is the very same. They are looking at it through the eyes of what they already believe. Your old thoughts act like a window that changes the color of everything you see through it.

If the old thoughts say the thing is bad we may see the new thing as bad. Our head does this because it wants to keep things simple and not have to start each time. So the thoughts we have before act as a way that lets some things of the new thing in and stops other things. This can make us see some things and not see other things. expected_answer: First, we must look at how people start their lives. Some people start with many things like money and good homes. To make things right, we must look at these starting points and try to make them more the same. question: what it means for a case to be put together well
expected_answer:
When you want to show that something is true, you must build a path of thoughts that leads a person to believe you. To do this well, you must start with things that everyone knows are true. These are your first pieces of the path. If your first pieces are not true, the whole path will fall down.

Second, you must use these true things to reach your next thought. Each new thought must follow from the one before it in a way that makes sense. If you jump from one thing to a different thing without a way to get there, your path is broken. People will look at the empty space between the thoughts and stop believing you. You must show how the first thing makes the second thing happen.

Third, you must look for things that might show you are wrong. If someone shows you a piece of information that goes against your path, you cannot just hide it. You must talk about it. You must show why that piece of information does not break your path. If you ignore the things that make you look wrong, people will not trust your work.

The thing after the third thing, you must make sure your path is strong and does not have holes. A hole is a place where a person can ask why and you have no answer. You must be able to answer every why. You must show that your thoughts are not just what you want to be true, but what the world shows is true.

Finally, the end of your path must be the thing you wanted to show. If your path is built well, a person who follows it from the start will reach the end and feel that they have no choice but to believe you. They will see that the path was easy to follow, the pieces were true, and the way you moved from one piece to the next was the only way to go.
english_query: what it means for a case to be put together well
ugf_response: First, we see that a case is a group of facts or ideas that are placed in a shape so a person can see what is happening. Then we notice that when the parts are put in a clear order, when the words fit the ideas, and when the important parts are not hidden, the case is put together well. This means that the person reading can follow the line of thought, can know why each part matters, and can see the point that the writer wants to make. A case that is put together well will have a start that tells what is being looked at, a middle that shows the facts and how they link, and an end that tells what we can learn or do. It will not jump around or leave out key parts, and it will use words that match the ideas. When all of this happens, the reader can understand the case without needing extra help.

This means the reader can trace the logical chain, understand the relevance of each premise, and see the intended conclusion. (2) An argument that is logically structured will have a descriptive premise, an intermediate conclusion, and an educational conclusion. It will not jump around or exclude key premises, and it will use logical statements. Once all of this happens, the player can interpret the argument without needing extra support.

question: probabilistic conditional, modus_ponens — child_rearing
expected_answer:
There is a kind of change that many people see that often works in how young people who read move through the steps of books for kids, and it is good to see it not as a tall thing that kids climb at set ages but as a step by step change that comes from the more they have read as well as how old they are.  

The kid who has been reading by them for two or more years — reading by choice, reading for fun, reading long enough times to really finish books and come back for more — makes a strong want for story that the first short books stop making happy.  

First short books are made to help new readers who read by them: short parts, few people, easy to guess story lines, things that end fast.  

For a kid with two years of reading by them behind them, those parts stop being help and start feeling slow. The story line is too easy, the parts too short, the end too sure too fast.  

What they want — what they are ready for — is a story line that needs them to keep things in mind over many parts, people who have inside thoughts that make the story more hard, problems that need a real path to end.  

That is the place that books for older kids fill.  

Our boy has been reading by himself since six, taking books and finishing them and asking for the next one, and he is now eight. Two whole years of easy reading by himself for fun.  

The same idea works for him. He will probably have already grown out of the first short books now, even if we keep getting them, and he will probably like more the kind of story and people that books for older kids with doing or make believe give.  

We should change our book place choices in that way.

In other words the case says we have a situation where the first thing happens and we think the second thing should happen because the first thing usually leads to the second.

The second idea is what the case thinks follows from that claim. It uses the way we think about “if one thing happens then another thing will happen” and the fact that we have seen the first thing. From those two pieces it says we can say that the child will start talking a lot. question: why sharing all you know is not always the best move
expected_answer:
When you know something that can change how things work, you might think that telling everyone is the best thing to do. You might think that if everyone knows the same thing, the whole world will become better and faster. However, there are many reasons why keeping some things to yourself can be a good choice.

First, we must think about how people use what they learn. If you tell someone a way to make things work better, they might use that way to help others. But they might also use it to hurt others or to take things that do not belong to them. If what you know can be used to do bad things, then telling everyone might cause more pain than good.

Second, we must think about how much work it takes to learn something. It takes a long time and a lot of hard work to find out how something works. If you give that work away for nothing, you might not have the power or the money to find out more things later. If people can just take what you know without doing the work themselves, they might stop trying to learn on their own. This could make the whole world stop growing and stop finding new ways to live.

Third, there is the problem of how people act when they get new information. Not everyone will use new information in a good way. Some people might get scared or angry when they learn something new. This can lead to fighting or to people making bad choices because they do not understand the thing you told them.

Finally, we must think about how much a person can hold in their head. If you try to tell everyone every single thing you know, the information can get all together in a way that is not clear. It can become a state where no one knows what is true and what is not. When too much information comes at once, people often stop listening or they start to believe things that are not true.

So, while sharing can be good, you must think carefully. You must look at who will hear you, how they will use it, and if the world is ready to hear it. Sometimes, the best way to help is to wait and to share only with those who can use it to do good.
english_query: why sharing all you know is not always the best move
ugf_response: First, we think about why you might want to tell all the facts you have. Then we see that sometimes when you tell all the facts, other people may feel bad or may not want to act, and this can make them stop listening. Also, sometimes keeping some facts for later can help you keep power or keep a plan safe. Also, if you share everything at once, the other people may not have time to think about each part and they may miss the important parts. So it can be better to choose what to share and when. This is why sharing all you know is not always the best move.
english_response: First, think about why you might want to share everything. Then see that sometimes sharing can lead to defection or defection, leading to selfcensorship. Likewise, sometimes keeping some information for later rounds can help you maintain authority or protect a strategy. Likewise, sharing everything at once can leave the others limited time to think about each step and miss critical details. Hence, sharing everything you know is not always the best move.

question: counterexample to: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P
expected_answer: One might say that a person can be said to have a must do thing when the person had a must do thing, the bad thing would not have happened if the must do thing had been done, and the not doing was the only thing that made the bad thing happen. The first two parts show the main idea of a must do thing for not doing: there is a must do thing and the bad thing would change if the must do thing was not done. The third part, saying it must be the only thing that made the bad thing happen, might look like it makes us point to the right person. But this part is too hard. Think of a person who works to watch the water at a water place and does not save a person who is going down in the water. The person who watches the water had a must do thing, and the person in the water would have lived if the person who watches the water had stepped in. But the bad thing had many helping things: the place had a not seen far down water part, another worker did not put up a sign that says bad, and a person who was there and could have thrown a thing that keeps a person up did not act. The not doing of the person who watches the water was not the only thing – it was one of many things happening at the same time. The third part does not work. Still no group of people who decide would clear the person who watches the water just because other things also helped the person go down. The law often says a person can be said to have a must do thing for not doing even when that not doing is needed but not enough by itself, because other things also help. The idea that asks for only one cause would end up keeping safe any person whose not doing happened together with other helping things – an end that would take away most of the law power for not doing.

So the first idea is partly right and partly not right.  

The second idea says that because there is a case where the not doing meets those conditions, that means the idea works for all other situations. In other words, they think that one case shows the whole idea is always correct.  

The thinking in the second idea is not good because a single case does not prove that the idea works for every case. The move from one case to all cases skips the need to check many other times, and that makes the reasoning weak. It would be better to look at many different cases before saying the idea is always true.

In fact, a defection can sometimes be a necessary condition for punishment, but it is not a sufficient one in every state. Therefore, the premise is partially true and partly false. The premise states that because there is a state where the defection meets those conditions, it implies the rule applies to all other states. question: counterexample to: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposi
expected_answer: Many people think that a piece of talk says an added idea when the truth of that idea is taken as true in the talk, and when the other piece of talk also takes it as true, and if the added idea is not true then the piece of talk is not true and not not true. question: material conditional, modus_ponens — deontological
expected_answer:
The thought that we have to do the right thing says that some things that people do make a need to do something no matter what comes after. Saying you will do something is the main thing.
