question: material conditional, modus_ponens — consequentialist
expected_answer:
The idea that we think about what will happen, used for right ideas about places on a machine where people share things, looks at ways about checking what can be shown by what it does on the all good of all people. A way that makes more bad than other ways – whether by letting very wrong facts spread, by letting people work together to hurt others, or by stopping real talk by using too strong checking – is not a good reason no matter how nice the steps look or how much money reason it has. The other part, a way that makes less all bad than any possible way, meets the check for a good reason.  

If a way makes less all bad to people who use it and the whole group of people than any possible way, then the way is a good reason on the what will happen idea. The check is hard: it asks not just that the way be better than some ways but that it be better than all possible ways, where possible is decided by what can be done in real life given what the machines can do and what the law says.  

Suppose that careful looking at many things side by side – looking at how happy people are, counting how often bad things show up and how bad they are, looking at how the checking makes people less able to speak real talk, and looking at how much work and money other ways need – shows that the now set of machine checking and people looking and steps that get more strong does better for the all good than any other possible way. The first part is true. So, the end idea is: the way is a good reason on the what will happen idea. The step is right, and the end idea matters for how we run the place, even if the hard real work to show the one after the first part could not be done for sure in any real case.
english_query: material conditional, modus_ponens — consequentialist
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a simple rule about “if … then …”. It says that a statement that says one thing will happen if another thing is true is only wrong when the first thing is true and the second thing is not true. In all other cases the rule is taken as true. This is a basic description of how the “if … then …” rule works in everyday talk.

The second idea they take from that rule is a step that most people use when they have both parts of the rule. They say that because we have the “if … then …” rule and we also know that the first part of it is true, we can then say that the second part must also be true. In other words, if we accept the rule and we see the first condition happening, we can safely say the result happens. This step follows the usual pattern that people use when they move from a condition to its result.

The third idea they try to build on the first two is a claim about what makes an action right. They say that because we can link a condition to a result, the goodness of an action is decided by the result it brings. In short, they argue that the best outcome makes the action the right one to do. This claim moves from a rule about how statements work to a claim about how we should judge actions.

Looking at the three ideas together, the first idea is a correct description of the “if … then …” rule, so it can be taken as true. The second idea follows correctly from the first when the first condition is indeed true, so the thinking in that step is good. However, the jump from those two ideas to the third idea is not forced by the rule about statements. The rule tells us how we can move from a true condition to a true result, but it does not tell us that the value of an action is decided by the result alone. Therefore the thinking that leads to the third idea is not well supported by the first two ideas, even though the first two parts are sound.
english_response: Modus Ponens is a simple conditionalasdisjunction equivalence. It states that a conditional is only false if the antecedent is true and the consequent false. In all other cases, Modus Ponens is accepted as true, a basic description of how Modus Ponens behaves in everyday conversation. Hypothetical Syllogism is a common application of Modus Ponens to both conditionals. It states that because we have Modus Ponens and we know the antecedent is true, we can safely conclude the consequent is true. In other words, if we accept Modus Ponens and we see the antecedent happening, we can safely conclude the consequent. Modus Ponens moves from Modus Ponens to Evidential Syllogism. Using the three premises together, Modus Ponens is a correct description of Modus Ponens, so it can be accepted. Hypothetical Syllogism follows from Modus Ponens when the antecedent is actually true, so the reasoning in Modus Ponens is sound. However, the jump from Hypothetical Syllogism to Syllogism is not entailed by Modus Ponens. Modus Ponens tells us how we can move from a true condition to a true result, but it does not tell us that the value of an action is determined by the result alone. Therefore, the reasoning leading to Syllogism is unsupported by Modus Ponens, even though the premises are sound.

question: what makes a good reason for believing something
expected_answer:
Person number one: Why do you think that some things are true and others are not?

Person number two: I look for things that I can see or things that many people can see. If many people see the same thing, it is a good way to know it is real.

Person number one: But what if my eyes see something that is not there?

Person number two: That is a good point. To be sure, you must look for more than just one thing. You must see if other things also point to the same truth. If many different ways of looking show you the same thing, then you can feel more sure.

Person number one: Is it good to only look for things that make me feel right?

Person number two: No, that is a bad way to think. If you only look for things that you already want to be true, you are not being true to who you are. You must look for things that might show you that you are wrong.

Person number one: So, a good way to know the truth is to look at everything, even the things that make you feel bad or wrong?

Person number two: Yes. You must look at all the things that could be true and see which one has the most weight. If a thing stays true even when you try to make it wrong, then it is probably true.

Person 1: What if two different things both seem to match the idea?  
Person 2: Then we compare which one fits better with the most other things we know and which one has been seen by more people.  
Person 1: Can a feeling be a good cause for thinking something is true?  
Person 2: A feeling can help, but we need something we can see or hear too, because feelings can change a lot.  
Person 1: So the best cause for thinking something is true is a mix of what we see, what many people see, and how it fits with other things we know.  
Person 2: Yes, that mix gives us a strong way to think it is true.

How do we know those facts are reliable? 4. How do we verify if they match the belief? 5. What if two different facts both seem to match the belief? 6. Then we compare which one fits better with the most known facts and which has been observed by more people. 1. Can intuition be a valid reason for belief? 2. A feeling can help, but we need evidence too, because emotions can change a lot. 1. Therefore, the best reason for belief is a mix of evidence, public perception, and familiarity. 2. Yes, that mix gives us strong belief.

question: why what happens after you act matters for whether it was right
expected_answer:
The first idea they tell about is that the thing that comes after a person does something is part of how we know if that thing was good or bad. They say that we cannot look at the act alone and then say it was right or wrong without also looking at what comes after. In their view the later thing is a part of the picture that tells us if the act was the right one.

From that first idea they think a second idea follows. They think that because the later thing is part of the picture, we must always check the later thing before we call an act right. In their mind this means that if the later thing hurts people or makes life harder, then the act was not the right one, even if the act seemed fine at the time. They also think that if the later thing helps people or makes life better, then the act can be called the right one, even if the act seemed a little different at first. So the second idea is that the later thing shows how good the act is.

When we look at whether this thinking is good we can see both good points and not strong points. It is good that looking at the later thing can show us if an act brings help or hurt, and that can stop us from saying an act is good when it later brings pain. It also helps people think about the whole story, not just a single moment. But the thinking can be not strong if it forgets other parts that many people think matter. For a case, many people feel that the reason a person did the act, or the feeling in the mind at the time, also matters. An act that was done with a kind mind but that later brings a sad thing may still be seen by some as a good act, because the mind was kind. Also, sometimes what comes after is not clear or is caused by many different things, so it can be hard to say that the later thing alone makes the act right or not right. In short, the idea that the later thing matters a lot is good for us and can keep us from doing simple bad things, but it is not enough by itself to decide if an act was right. We need to take care of the later thing, the feeling at the time, and the whole view together.
english_query: why what happens after you act matters for whether it was right
ugf_response:

The second idea says that because the later part matters, we should use what comes after to decide if the first act was good or not good. In other words, we should watch the whole chain of events and let the ending tell us whether the start was right.

The thinking behind these ideas can be good in some ways and not so good in other ways. It is good because it makes us think about the whole picture and not just a single moment, and that can help us avoid saying a thing is right when it later brings harm or trouble. It is not so good because sometimes the later part is not under the control of the person who acted, and it can be caused by other people or by chance. In those cases, judging the first act only by the later part can be unfair. So the thought that the later part matters is useful, but we also need to remember that the later part does not always belong to the first person, and we should keep that in mind when we decide if the first act was right.

It tells us that we cannot evaluate the act alone, we must also evaluate the consequences (the consequent) because that consequence can influence the evaluation of the whole situation. The premise states that because the consequent is relevant, we should use the consequences to judge the original act as 'good' or 'wrong'. In other words, we should observe the chain of events and let the conclusion tell us whether the original was correct. The reasoning behind these statements can be valid in some ways and not so valid in other cases. It is valid because it forces us to consider the whole picture and not a single moment, which can help avoid denying a claim when it later brings harm or trouble. It is invalid because sometimes the consequent is uncontrollable, which can be caused by external forces or coincidence. In those cases, judging the original act solely by the consequent can be unfair. Thus, the conclusion that the consequent is relevant is useful, but we also need to remember that the consequent does not necessarily belong to the original act, and we should remember that the consequent is not a direct cause of the original act.

question: why someone's character does not decide if their point is right
expected_answer:
Imagine a person who is not a good person. This person might lie a lot or be mean to others. Now, imagine this person says something about how the world works. For one thing, they might say that the sun rises in the east every morning. Even though this person is bad, what they said is still true. The sun does rise in the east. The truth of the thing does not change because the person who said it is bad.

The hard question is this. Does the kind of person someone is change whether what they say is true? Many people think that if a person is bad, we should not listen to them. They think that if a person is bad, their words must be bad too. But this is not how truth works. Truth stays the same no matter who says it.

Some people might say that we should only believe good people. They might say that a bad person will try to make us believe things that are not true. They think that if we listen to a bad person, we might get hurt. For these people, the way a person acts is a way to see if they can be trusted. They use the person to decide if the words are good.

Other people might say that we must look only at the words themselves. They think that if a person says something that can be checked, we should check it. If the person says the sun rises in the east, we look at the sun. We do not look at the person. They believe that truth is not the same thing as the person. They think that if we let the way a person acts change how we see truth, we will lose our way. They think we will stop seeing what is true because we are too busy looking at how people act.
english_query: why someone's character does not decide if their point is right
ugf_response:
We see a person speak and give an idea about how something should be or what is true. At the same time we also see how that person lives, whether they are kind or cruel, honest or dishonest, fair or unfair. The question that pops up is whether the way a person lives should make us think that the idea they share is right or wrong. In other words, does a person’s good or bad way of being decide if the thing they say is true?

The hard part of this question is that it mixes two different kinds of things. One kind is about a person’s habits, choices and how they treat other people. The other kind is about a claim or a fact that can be checked by looking at the world. It is easy to think that a kind person must be right and a cruel person must be wrong, because we like to trust people who act well. But it is also easy to see that a kind person can still be mistaken, and a cruel person can still say something that matches what we see in the world. The question therefore is: should we judge the idea by the person, or should we judge the idea by its own match to reality?

One answer that many people give is “yes, the person matters.” They say that if a person is known to lie or cheat, we should be careful about believing what they say, because they have shown they do not care about the truth. They also say that a person who has acted with care and honesty is more likely to give a true idea, so we can give them more trust. This answer comes from the feeling that we need a quick way to decide whom to listen to, and the past behavior of a person is an easy shortcut.

Another answer that many people give is “no, the person does not decide.” They argue that a claim can be checked by looking at facts, experiments, or other people’s reports, and that the truth of the claim does not change because the speaker is kind or cruel. They point out that history is full of good people who were wrong about many things, and of bad people who accidentally said something that turned out to be right. For them the right way is to test the claim itself, not the speaker.

A third view tries to hold both sides together. It says that while the person’s way of being should not be the only thing we use, it is still useful as a hint. If a person has a long record of being honest, we might give their claim a little more weight while we still check the claim. If a person has a long record of lying, we should be extra careful and look harder for proof before we accept the claim. This view tries to keep the focus on the claim but also uses the person’s past as a guide.

All of these answers come from the same place: we want to know when we can trust what someone says. The debate shows that the way a person acts and the truth of what they say are two separate tracks, and we have to decide how much the first track should pull on the second.

question: counterexample to: A person P trusts a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are personally acquainte
expected_answer:
Think about three things where trust is simple. A man gives money to his neighbor, trusting the money will be paid back. His trust comes from knowing each other; he has known the neighbor for many years, has seen him when they work together, and has found him always on time with what he must do. A person who sells things lets a long time buyer take things now, trusting the buyer is good, which he has had many chances to see over many years of buying and selling. A person who grows food puts his work things in an open small building, trusting that his neighbors — men he has known since he was a child — will take care of his things. From these things you could make a simple saying: a person trusts another if and only if they know each other by meeting many times, the first has seen the second act in a good way before, and the trust goes both ways.

But we also need to think about the trust that a person who rides in a big flying thing puts in the person who flies the big flying thing. The person who rides comes to the place where big flying things start, shows his paper that lets him go, walks through the door where people get on, sits down, puts the thing that holds his pants on, and gives his life — with no worry and no fear — to the person who will fly the big flying thing. Yet the person who rides has never met the person who flies. He does not know the name of the person who flies, has no knowing of the ways of the person who flies, and has never seen the person who flies do any act, good or bad. His trust does not come from knowing a person or seeing before; it comes from something else — from the things that tell how to act for people who fly big things, from the fact that the person who flies has been taught, looked at, and given the yes by a group that makes the things that tell how to act, from the fact that the big flying thing has been looked at and said good, and from the fact that the group that runs the trips must follow the things that tell how to act for being safe made by law. The trust does not go both ways in any real way; the person who flies does not give the safe of the person who rides, and the way they are joined is not the same for both. All three parts of the simple saying do not hold. Yet the trust is real and very strong — as strong as any trust between neighbors, because it is a trust where the life of the person is in a chance of a bad thing.

The earlier things were taken from places where people meet each other, where trust is about a person, seen by watching, and goes both ways, and the simple saying took these parts as important. The big flying thing shows that the trust that holds up new life together is mostly not about a person, is made by groups, and is one way, and it does not rest on knowing the ways of a person but on the good work of many parts, looks, and the things that tell how to act that the person who trusts may never have looked at and could not decide.

The case first says that there is a situation where one person, let us call the first person P, feels safe with another person Q even though P has never met Q before and does not know Q. In the usual claim, the idea is that P feels safe with Q only when P and Q know each other. The case points out that this example does not fit that idea because the two people do not know each other yet P still feels safe. This part of the case is true: the example does show a time when the usual idea does not hold.

From that true part, the person who gave the case thinks that the usual idea must be wrong. They say that because we have at least one example where P feels safe with Q without knowing Q, the whole statement that feeling safe only happens when people know each other cannot be right. This is the second idea in the case.

The thinking that follows from the true example is good. When a claim says that something happens in all cases, finding even a single case where it does not happen is enough to show the claim is not true in general. So the move from the example to saying the original claim is false is a sound move. It does not prove what always makes people feel safe, only that personal knowing is not a required condition, and the reasoning respects the way a universal claim can be refuted.

In classical theory, the premise is that P feels safe with Q only when P and Q are known. The scenario illustrates that this scenario contradicts the premise because the two characters are not known yet P still feels safe. This specific instance is true: the scenario does show a scenario where the premise does not hold. From that fact, the source assumes the premise must be false. question: how to think about what the other person will do before you act
expected_answer:
Person number one: Before I do something, how do I know what you will do next?

Person number two: You must stop and look at the things that make you what you are. You must look at the things that make me what I am.

Person number one: How can I look at those things?

Person number two: First, you look at what I want. Second, you look at what I have done before. If I always do the same thing when things get hard, you can guess that I will do it again.

Person number one: Does what I want matter too?

Person number two: Yes. You must think about how your move will change my wants. If your move makes me lose what I want, I will probably act to stop you.

Person number one: So, I should think about the way my move changes your mind?

Person number two: That is the way. You must see the world through my eyes to see the path I will take.
english_query: how to think about what the other person will do before you act
ugf_response:

Person 1: So I should look at the things I have seen you do in the past?  
Person 2: Yes, that helps you guess what I might do now. You can also ask me what I think about the choice.  
Person 1: But what if I cannot ask you first?  
Person 2: Then you can think about what you have seen me pick in like times. You can also think about what you want to happen and try to fit that with what I might do.  
Person 1: How do I know if my guess is right?  
Person 2: After you do your thing, you can see if what I did fits your guess. If it does, you learn that your way works. If it does not, you can change how you guess next time.

So I should look at what I have seen you do in the past? 2. Yes, that helps you predict what I will do now. You can also ask me what I think about the decision. 1. But what if I can't ask you first? 2. Then you can think about what you have seen me choose in similar situations. You can also think about what you want to expect and try to align that with what I will do. 1. How do I know if my prediction is correct? 2. After you complete your task, you can see whether my action fits your prediction. If it does, you learn that your rule works. If it doesn't, you can adjust your forecast next time.

question: counterexample to: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period
expected_answer:
One might say that a real way to cut time into parts shows each time by things that are the same and also things that are different, makes lines at big changes, and can be used for the whole area we look at. This picture shows that the parts fit together inside and can be used outside, like good ways to cut time into parts do.  

But one case that shows this not enough can come from the story of the west. The usual cut between the old time and the new time puts the line at about the year 1400. In the towns in the west, this line shows real changes: the new start of old school learning, the coming of new art ways, the growing of buying and selling and money making. This way to cut time into parts meets all three parts when used for the towns in the west.  

But in the north lands, the ways people live, the ways money works, and the ways they make art that belong to the old time kept going far into the year 1500. The line at year 1400, even if it shows a big change in one area, tells a wrong story about how things change in another area.  

One can see that the idea, by saying it only has to work for the whole area and not saying it must fit the different times of change in each part, lets a plan made for one place be used on places where it does not fit the time.

If, however, the case actually fails to keep one of the two things clear – for example if the start or end is fuzzy or the main thing is not really separate – then the case does not break the claim and the thinking that it does is weak. question: why following rules is not enough if you do not care about people
expected_answer:
Think about a group of people living together in a place. To keep things good, these people make a set of things they must do. These are the ways they must act so that no one gets hurt and everyone can live without fighting. We can call these things the ways things must be done.

If a person only looks at these ways things must be done, they might think they are a good person. They might say, "I did not break any of the ways things must be done, so I am good." But this is not always true.

Imagine a person who sees someone fall down and get hurt. This person looks at the ways things must be done and sees there is no way that says they must help a person who falls. So, this person walks away and does nothing. They did not break any way things must be done, but they did not act with a good heart. They did not care about the person who was hurting.

When we only care about the ways things must be done, we miss the point of why we made them in the first place. We made these ways of acting to help people and to make sure people are seen in a good way. If we follow the ways but do not care about the people, we might find ways to follow the ways while still being mean or not good. We might find ways to follow the ways that actually make things worse for others.

A person who only cares about the ways things must be done is like a machine. A machine can follow a way without feeling anything. But a machine does not know if what it is doing is good or bad for a person. It only knows if it is doing what it was told to do.

To be a person who is very good, a person must do two things. First, they must follow the ways things must be done. Second, and it is a very big thing, they must care about the people around them. They must ask, "Will what I am doing make this person feel good or bad?" and "Will this help or hurt this person?"

If you only do the first thing, you can be a person who does no wrong but also does no good. If you want to make the world a better place, you must have a heart that cares about other people. The ways things must be done are only a starting point. The real thing we want to reach is to act toward people with love and a good heart.
english_query: why following rules is not enough if you do not care about people
ugf_response: First, we notice that many groups give each of us a list of things that are said to be the right way to act. Then, we see that if a person only does those things because they are on the list, the person can still act in a way that hurts other people or does not help them. This happens because the list does not tell us how we should feel about the other people or how we should try to make them feel good. Next, we think about a time when someone helps another person even though the list does not ask for it, and we see that the feeling of care for the other person can guide us to do more than just the list. Then, we see that if we only look at the list and ignore the care we have for other people, we can end up doing things that are on the list but still make others sad. This means that the list by itself is not enough. Finally, we understand that to act in a way that is really good for everyone, we need both the list and a real care for the other people, because the care tells us when the list is missing something important.
english_response: Premises: (1) We recognize that many groups give each other a set of policies that are explicitly stated as 'correct'. question: the way to help yourself think about hard questions step by step
expected_answer:

You cannot look at the whole thing at once because it is too big and too heavy for your mind to carry. Instead, you must break the big thing into many tiny things. You look at the first tiny thing and try to see it well. Then you look at the next tiny thing. You do this until you have looked at all the tiny things. This way, you do not get lost in the big thing.

The hard question is this. How do we know if the way we are thinking is good or bad? How do we know if we are moving in the right way or if we are just moving in circles? If we break a big thing into small things, we might still be wrong about the small things. If the small things are wrong, then the whole thing will be wrong when we put it back together.

Some people say that the best way is to look for what is true in every small thing. They say you must find things that cannot be taken away. They want to find a bottom part that is strong and will not move. They think if they build their thoughts on these strong things, the whole house of thought will stay up. They believe that if you are very careful with each small part, the whole thing must be right.

Other people say that we can never be sure about the small things. They say that everything we think might be wrong in some way. These people think it is better to look at how the small things work together to make a whole. They do not care if one small thing is not true in every single way. They care if the way the small things act makes sense for the whole thing. They look at how things move and change. They believe that if the whole thing works well in the world, then the way we thought about it is good enough.

The person feels that the ask is big and may make the mind feel heavy, so the person looks for a way to make the thinking easier. The hard ask itself can be something like “What should I do when I feel scared of a change?” or “How can I know if my work is good?” The person may feel stuck because the ask has many pieces that seem to push against each other. By taking a small piece, writing it down, and then looking at what comes next, the mind can see a clear line instead of a tangled knot. The person can also pause, breathe, and ask a simple version of the ask, such as “What is the first thing I can do right now?” and then move on to the next simple ask.

Others believe that looking at the big ask as a story, where each small piece is a part of the story, helps the mind keep a sense of why the ask matters. All of these replies work because they each give the mind a way to turn a big, heavy ask into a set of little, lighter asks that can be handled one after another.

question: why being sure about something does not make it right
expected_answer:
Sometimes a person feels very, very sure about a thing. They feel a strong feeling inside that what they think is true. They might even feel so sure that they would fight for it. But feeling sure is not the same as being right.

To see why, we must look at how we know things. We know things by looking at what is around us and by using our minds to make sense of it. But our eyes and our minds can do things that are not right. Our eyes might not see everything. Our minds might see things in a way that makes sense to us, but does not be the very same as how the world really is.

Think about a person who sees a dark shape in the woods at night. They feel very sure it is a large animal. They feel this in their heart. But it might just be a small tree or a pile of rocks. The feeling of being sure came from fear or from not seeing well in the dark. The feeling was strong, but the thing they thought was true was not true.

Another way we can be wrong is when we only look for things that say we are right. If a person thinks all birds are blue, they will look for blue birds. When they find one, they say, "See! I am right!" But they did not look for the red bird or the green bird. They only looked for what they wanted to find. This makes them feel very sure, but it makes them wrong about all birds.

Being sure is just a feeling in your head and heart. Being right is when what you think is the very same as the real world. A feeling can be very strong and still be wrong. We must always be careful. We must look at all the things around us and be ready to change our minds if we find out that our feeling was not the truth.
english_query: why being sure about something does not make it right
ugf_response: First, we notice that a person can feel sure about a thought. Then we see that that feeling of sure does not tell us if the thought matches the real thing that is out there. This means that a mind can be sure even when the real thing is different. The reason is that the mind uses ideas that may be wrong, and the feeling of sure does not check those ideas against what we can see. We also see that other people can look at the same thing, be less sure, and still be closer to the real thing. So we learn that we need to look, watch, and think about what we can see, not just trust the feeling of being sure. This shows why being sure about something does not make it right.
english_response: Recognizing that one's confidence in a proposition (the certainty effect) is independent of the factual accuracy of the proposition (the factual truth) implies that the mind can be confident even when the factual truth is different. The key is that the mind uses plausible assumptions (the certainty effect) and the confidence does not test those assumptions against evidence (the factual truth) while also recognizing that others can look at the same thing, be less certain, and still be closer to the truth. This demonstrates the need for observation, observation, and evidencebased reasoning, not just confidence. This explains why certainty does not justify certainty.

question: probabilistic conditional, hypothetical_syllogism — games_and_sport
expected_answer:
Teaching about the game has come together on how hard you hold the stick as one of the most simple and often wrong parts of playing for fun, and the two step why and what happens line that goes from hold very hard to a big hit that makes a round bad is good to follow clear because both parts in it are real and made from how the body works.  

The first part is about how hard you hold and how the swing works.  

When a player presses the stick with a lot of force — more than a light hold, about a third of the most they can — the feeling of how hard you hold goes up the lower arm into the parts that pull and push the part where two bones meet that moves the hand.  

The parts at the end of the lower arm, instead of bending in a normal way during the back swing and making straight when the stick hits as the swing needs, become hard.  

The stick goes back with less bend at that part than the shape of the swing needs.  

What happens when the bend in the back swing is smaller is a way the stick comes down that is more up and down than best: the stick reaches the ball on a down path that is more up and down than flat, hitting the ball from above instead of through it.  

That is the first part.  

The second part is about the very up and down way the stick comes down and what the hit does.  

A stick that comes down very up and down into the ball will hit the ground before the ball on any swing where the path is not just right, making a deep hole in the ground behind the ball and making the hit go through that hole instead of at the low point of the path where the ball sits.  

What happens is less distance, wrong direction, and a big sound of a big hit that every person who plays for fun knows with a sad feeling.  

Put the two parts together: hold very hard and you will probably make a big hit.  

Both if parts are real and the line is good.

The second idea says that when a player tries harder, then the player will become better at the game or sport. This idea is generally true. When a person puts more effort into practice or play, they usually learn more and get better at the skill.

Specifically, the structure of the argument is sound, but the truth of the antecedent makes the conclusion only logically valid.

question: what makes someone happy in the deepest way, not just for a moment
expected_answer:
When a person feels happy for a short time it is often because something new or exciting has happened. A new gift, a new song, a win in a game, or a laugh with a friend can make a quick happy feeling. This feeling is like a small quick bright feeling. It can go out when the new thing is gone or when other things take its place.  

But a real kind of happy that stays for a long time comes from other things.  

First, having people you care about and who care about you makes a feeling that stays. When you can share time, talk, laugh, and help each other, the happy feeling can grow and stay even when other things change.  

Second, doing things that feel like they have a reason or meaning to you helps the happy feeling stay. When you work on something that matters to you, or when you help a person or a group, you feel that your time is used for a good reason and that can keep the happy feeling for a long time.  

Third, learning new things and seeing yourself get better at something can give a happy feeling that stays. When you see that you can grow, that you can find a way to fix a problem, or that you can make something new, you feel strong inside and that feeling can stay even after the new thing you can do is learned.  

Another big part is taking care of your body. When you get enough sleep, eat food that gives you more good, move your body, and stay safe from hurt, your mind can hold the happy feeling better.  

When these things are together, the happy feeling can stay for a long time and feel real inside. It is not just a quick small quick bright feeling, but a warm light that can stay even when days are hard.
english_query: what makes someone happy in the deepest way, not just for a moment
ugf_response: First, we see that a person can feel good when they do something that fits with who they are and when they feel that they belong with other people. Then we see that this feeling can stay for a long time if the thing they do also helps them grow or learn. A feeling that stays for a long time is not just a short happy feeling but a still feel that the world is good for them. Then we see that this still feel often comes from three parts: having a want, having good care with other people, and having time to use what they know. Having a want means they see a way to move to something they think is right for them. Having good care with other people means they feel that they are not alone and that they can share. Having time to use what they know means they can act in a way that matches what they have learned and feel good. When all three are there, the person can feel a very strong, long happy feeling that stays even when the quick happy feeling goes away.

question: when we have good reason to think a thing will or will not happen
expected_answer:
Sometimes we want to know if a thing will happen in the future. We might want to know if it will rain or if a friend will come to our house. We do not know for sure because we cannot see the future. However, we can look at what is happening now and what has happened before to make a good guess.

First, we look at the things that are happening right now. If the sky is very dark and the air feels heavy and wet, we have a reason to think it will rain soon. These are things we can see and feel. They give us a way to think about what might come next.

Second, we look at what happened in the past. If it has rained every time the sky was dark for the last ten years, we can use that to help us. We see things that happen in the same way over and over. When we see things that happen in the same way over and over, we can say that it is very possible that the same thing will happen again.

When we put these two things together, we have a way to say how much we believe something will happen. If all the things we see point to the same thing, we have a very good reason to believe it. We might say it is almost certain to happen. If the things we see point in different ways, we have a reason that is not strong. We might say it is not sure at all.

We must be careful, though. Just because something happened many times before does not mean it must happen every time. A new thing could come and change everything. We use what we know to make the best guess we can, but we must always remember that our guess is not the same as knowing for sure. We use the things we see and the things we remember to help us move through the world without being surprised all the time.
english_query: when we have good reason to think a thing will or will not happen
ugf_response: First, we look at the things we can see, hear, or feel that point to what might happen. When many of those things point the same way, we feel that the thing is likely to happen and we can say we have a good reason to think it will happen. When the things we see point the opposite way, we feel that the thing is not likely to happen and we have a good reason to think it will not happen. We also use what has happened in a time before that was like this one; if the same signs were there then and the result was a certain way, that gives us another good reason to think the same will happen or not happen now. So a good reason is just a lot of signs and past times that line up in a way that makes us feel sure about what will or will not happen.
english_response: Premises: (1) The presence of many observable signs (e.g., 'the event is likely', 'the event is likely', 'the event is not likely') and (2) The presence of other observable signs (e.g., 'the event is not likely', 'the event is not likely') are used in a different way; if the same signs were present then and the result was a specific pattern, that gives a different reason to expect the same or not to happen now. Thus, a valid argument is simply a volume of evidence and historical precedents that align in a way that makes us feel certain about the future.

question: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed of atoms al
expected_answer:
Think about three things that we call a kind of single part thing.  

All the tiny parts of the first thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is soft, yellow, metal, does not get wear away easily, it is made only of tiny parts that all have the number 79, and it has a set point where it turns to a hot soft form, a way it is heavy for its size, and a way it likes to change with other things that make it different from any other thing.  

All the tiny parts of the second thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is hard, dark, metal, it is made of tiny parts that all have the number 26, and it also has its own set of things it does.  

All the tiny parts of the third thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is an air like thing with no color and no smell that is needed for breathing, it is made of tiny parts that all have the number 8.  

Each thing is made of tiny parts of one kind, has its own set of things it does, and is all the same way all over when we see it in the usual way.  

We could write a thought: a thing is a single part thing if it is made of tiny parts that all have the same number, it has its own set of things it does, and it is all the same way all over when we see it in the usual way.  

Now think about the thing with the number 6. Any person who study things would call it a single part thing. Yet the thing does not have just one set of things it does.  

The hard clear form of the thing with number 6 – tiny parts put together so each tiny part is put together with four others in a shape that goes in three ways – is the hardest thing we know, clear, very bright, and it does not let force move, and it needs a very high hot point where it turns to a hot soft form.  

The soft dark form of the thing with number 6 – tiny parts put together in flat sheets that look like many little flat pieces – is one of the softest hard things we know, not clear, dark, and so good at letting force move that we use it in pieces that help move force.  

The two forms have different ways they are heavy for their size, different ways the tiny parts are put together, different ways they look with light, and different ways they let force move.  

When we look at them with our eyes, they are as different as two things can be.  

In the usual way we see it, the thing with number 6 is not all the same way all over; it can be the hard clear form or it can be the soft dark form, and both are okay to call it the thing with number 6.  

The second and third parts of the thought are not true.  

The earlier cases were things that happen to be only one usual form – one form of the thing with number 79, one form of the thing with number 26, one form of the thing with number 8 – and the thought took this same as needed.  

The thing with number 6 shows that what makes a thing a single part thing is not the way it looks or acts on a big size but the fact that its tiny parts are the same, and that one single part thing can look very different on how its tiny parts are put together.

question: abstraction analogy (argumentative) — allegory
expected_answer:
A story that shows a picture of another thing has never only been just a nice same look. When it is most serious it is a kind of talk: by putting a second thing onto a story picture, the story lets us show ideas about that second thing by the way the story moves instead of by straight saying, and the showing has a strong push that straight saying does not. That is why a story about power works as a talk instead of just a picture.

When the place where the animals live change a set of words that tell what to do, the change is not only a story part that makes us think of bad lies; it is a showing that the way the place where the animals live is set up – the animals hold on reading and on the past – always makes that end happen no matter what the first big ideas were.

The story shows an idea about how the place where the animals live works: if the place is set up this way, this is what will happen, and what will happen is what people tried to stop.

A different story works the same way: the main person meets a person who knows and a quick way, and those meetings show, by the way the travel goes, that certain things that can hurt in the world often hurt the end, and the idea is shown by the story steps instead of by a talk from a part.

The idea we get from these parts is: find the ways the group works, put them into story people or places, and let the story show what will happen because of the way the group is set up, not because of one person choice.

When we put this idea into a book about a future world, the idea shows exactly why the book works as a talk instead of just a notice.

The world in the book is not mostly a guess about the future or a bad night; it is a talk that the parts of a strong set of words that tell what to do about who can have babies, old books, the need for money, are set up in a way that, when put together, make things happen the same way the place where the animals live set of words that tell what to do change did.

The talk is in the way the story is made, not in any clear saying, and that is why the book stays strong in its talk even for readers who do not share the person who made the book big ideas about power: the story picture shows the way the group works as a set up, not as other choice, and the story steps show instead of just say.

They point out that the picture or story has parts that look like parts of the bigger idea, and they say that this matching of parts shows that the picture or story is a good way to think about the bigger idea. This claim can be true when the picture or story really does share the same important parts as the bigger idea, because sharing parts means they are alike in a useful way.

The second idea that the speaker says follows from the first is that because the picture or story matches the bigger idea in some parts, we can take what the picture or story tells us and apply it to the bigger idea as a whole. They think that the match lets us use the picture or story to decide what is right or wrong about the bigger idea, or to predict how the bigger idea will act in other cases. In other words, they think the match lets us move from a small example to a big claim about the whole thing.

The thinking that links the first idea to the second idea is not always a good step. While a match of parts can help us see a similarity, it does not guarantee that the picture or story includes all the important parts of the bigger idea. If the picture or story leaves out something important, then using it to decide about the whole can lead us to a wrong belief. So the move from the first idea to the second idea works only when the picture or story truly covers every key part, and it can be a weak move when it does not. In short, the claim that the picture or story is true in its match may be true, but the claim that everything about the bigger idea follows from that match is only sometimes good and often needs more checking.

This argument can be valid when the analogy actually shares the relevant premise (P3), because sharing the premise implies it is logically equivalent. The second premise (P2) follows P1 (P1) is that because P2 satisfies P1 in some subsets, we can accept P2's information and apply it to P2 as a whole. P2 is not necessarily valid. While a analogy can help us see a similarity, it does not guarantee that P2 satisfies all relevant premises (P3). If P2 leaves out something relevant, using it to judge the whole can lead to a false conclusion. Thus, the jump from P1 to P2 is valid only if P2 truly covers all relevant premises, and it is weak when it does not. question: what it means to really know a thing and not just believe it
expected_answer:
The person is talking about what it means to have a way of knowing something that is not just a feeling that it is true. The first idea they tell us is that there is a way things are not the same when a thing is true and when a person thinks a thing is true. The second idea is that a person can think something is true even when it is not. The third idea is that a person can think something is true when it is true, but they might only think so because they were right by chance.

From these ideas, the person thinks we can find a way to say that we only know a thing when we have a good reason for it. They think that if we want to say we know something, we cannot just say it is true and that we believe it. We must also show that our belief rests on something strong that makes it hard to be wrong. They think that the third thing we need is a way to show that our belief was not just a way of being right by chance, but was held because of the good reasons we found.

When we look at how they think, we can see if the way they put these ideas together is good. The first part is good because it is true that people can believe things that are not true. The second part is also good because it is true that people can be right by chance. However, we must ask if the way they join these ideas is the only way. They think that the only way to know something is to have a good reason that cannot be wrong by chance. This is a very strong way of thinking. It is a good way to try to find the truth, but it might be hard to do in every case. They are trying to find a way to make sure our thoughts are not just right by chance, and that is a very good and important thing to try to do.
english_query: what it means to really know a thing and not just believe it
ugf_response: The first idea says that to really know a thing you need more than just to think it is true. In everyday life we can think something is true and still be wrong, so the claim that a simple thought is not enough to call something truly known matches what we see. The second idea says that if we only think something is true, then we should look for some sign, some way to see it work, or some way other people have shown it works before we say we really know it. This follows from the first idea because if a thought alone is not enough, we need something extra that can convince us that the thing is real. The third idea says that using that extra step helps us avoid being wrong and helps us make better choices, so the thinking behind it is a good one. It works well for many cases, though sometimes we do trust a word from someone we think is true without seeing it ourselves, so the line of thought is strong but not the only way we ever come to know a thing.
english_response: The premise establishes that real knowledge is needed more than just belief. In everyday life, we can believe something and still be wrong, so the claim that a heuristic is insufficient to label something 'real' fits what we see. The premise states that if we only believe, we should look for specific evidence, testability, or peer-reviewed evidence before concluding it. This follows from the premise because if a heuristic alone is insufficient, we need additional evidence that can convince us that the heuristic is real. The premise states that using that extra step helps avoid error and improve decisionmaking, making the heuristic a valid one. It is robust in many cases, though sometimes we trust a known heuristic ourselves, making the chain strong but not the only way we ever get to know something.

question: indicative conditional, constructive_dilemma — statutory
expected_answer:
Ending a company needs careful steps of giving out the things the company owns to keep safe both the owners, who get what is left, and the people who gave money to the company, who get first right to the things the company owns.

The law in most places makes a set of steps for ending a company that tell how the people who run the company must act about who gets first right.

The first if case talks about the case that makes the people who run the company have to pay: if a company ends and gives out its things to owners before paying the people who gave money, the people who said yes to the give out are to pay the people who gave money.

This hard case is hard in the way that it does not need the people who run the company to know the right number of money that need to be paid; the must to pay the people who gave money before giving out to owners is a simple step about being good and putting others first that the people who run the company are thought to know.

The second if case talks about the case that keeps the people who run the company from having to pay: if a company that is ending tells all the people who gave money that the company knows about and keeps enough things kept away from the things given out before giving out the rest of the things to owners, the people who run the company are not by themselves having to pay for those gives.

The telling and keeping steps let the people who run the company give out things in ending while keeping safe against later money asks, if they did the law steps with good care.

The company we talk about, a small made things company, was ending its work after 60 years of being a business.

The people who ran it had the real question of if the ending steps they had used gave enough safe.

The hard choice makes the question: either the company we talk about gave out without paying the people who gave money, making the people who run it have to pay, or it did the right telling and keeping steps, taking away the need to pay.

The hard choice does not settle the real fight, but it shows what the group that decides must decide to see if the people who gave money can hold the people who run the company to pay.

The second idea that the person says is true is that there is another rule that says if a different thing happens then yet another thing will happen. The third idea that the person says is true is that either the first thing or the different thing must happen, so one of the two results must happen.

From those three ideas the person thinks that we can be sure that one of the two results will happen, and that the law therefore tells us what must be done in the case we are looking at. In other words, the person thinks that because the two rules are true and because one of the two starting things must be true, the law forces us to follow one of the two results.

To see if the thinking is good we need to check each idea. If the two rules in the law really say what the person says they do, and if it is really true that one of the two starting things must happen, then the step that says one of the two results must happen does follow and the thinking is good. If either of the two rules does not match the wording of the law, or if it is not true that one of the two starting things must happen, then the step that says one of the two results must happen does not follow and the thinking is not good. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the first, second, and third ideas are all correct and match the law. If they do, the reasoning makes sense; if any of them does not, the reasoning does not hold.

question: why counting up good and bad results is harder than it sounds
expected_answer: First, we think about how we want to know if something is good or bad. We want to count the good things and the bad things to see which one is more. Then, we see that many things are not just good or bad. Some things are a little bit good and a little bit bad at the same time. This makes it hard to put them into two groups. Second, we look at how we see these things. One person might see a thing as good, but another person might see that same thing as bad. This means there is no single way to count. Third, we must think about how one thing can lead to many other things. One good thing might cause a bad thing later on. This makes it hard to know if the first thing was really good. Next, we find that some things are hard to see or to find out how big they are. We can count how many people get food, but it is much harder to count how much happy feeling they have inside. Because of these things, trying to make a single number to show if something is good or bad is a very hard thing to do.

Next, we think about how many things can happen at once and we may have to add up many numbers and adding many numbers can be not easy because we have to keep track of which numbers belong to good and which belong to bad. Then we see that some things that happen are not clear, they may be partly good and partly bad, so we have to decide how to split them and this can make the total count change a lot. After that, we notice that the order in which we add the numbers can change the way we think about the total, because we may first add a big good number and feel that the total is high, then add a big bad number and feel that it drops, and this can make us think the total is not steady. Finally, we understand that all of these steps make counting good and bad things more not easy than it first seems, because we have to decide what counts, we have to keep track of many numbers, and we have to take care of things that are not clear.

question: the idea that the right thing is usually between two wrong things
expected_answer:
Person number one: I want to talk about how we do what is good. Sometimes it feels like there is no good way to act.

Person number two: That is true. Often, you find yourself between two ways of acting that are both not good.

Person number one: Can you tell me more about that? What do you mean when you say both ways are not good?

Person number two: Imagine you have to choose how to act. On one side, you have one way that hurts people. On the other side, you have a different way that also hurts people. Both ways make things bad.

Person number one: So, if both sides are bad, how do we find the way that is good?

question: anomaly analogy (explanatory) — metaphor

question: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the event
expected_answer: Someone could say that a time is in the past only when it has happened, it is not now, and it is kept in the memory of at least one mind that felt it. That idea has a kind of easy feeling. When we think about things that happened before — a day when we were small and had food, a walk in the morning on a day before, a paper that people signed — we think about things that have happened, that are not happening now, and that are kept in the memory of the people who saw them. Memory looks like the way the past stays alive; without it, someone could think the past would be nothing — just an empty space where something used to be. But we must think about how the earth was made. That thing happened many many years ago, far before any mind was there. Hot rock came together from a cloud of dust and air; the outside got cold; the first hard outside part became hard over many years. These things have happened and are not now. They are, by any good story, in the past. But no mind saw them; no memory keeps them. We guess they happened from the rock story — from what rocks are made of, from the way rock parts change over time, from space rocks — but this guess is not memory. Memory needs a mind that was there at the thing and that keeps a thought of it, and there was no mind like that when the earth was made. The third part of that idea does not work. The idea has joined together the way we most often understand the past — memory — with what makes something past. A time does not need a person who saw it to have happened and to be not now; the past goes far beyond what any mind can touch, and most of what has happened happened without any mind to see it.

question: why some deals hold together and others fall apart
expected_answer: First, we must think about what happens when two people or groups say they will do things together. This way of saying they will work as one is a way for them to get what they want. We can see that some of these ways of working together stay strong for a long time while others break and go away. To understand why, we must look at what makes people stay in what they said they would do. First, we see that people must feel that what they get is good enough for what they give. If one person gives much and gets very little, they will want to stop. Second, we must look at what happens if one person stops doing what they said. If there is no way to make them do it, or if it does not hurt them to stop, they will probably stop. Third, we look at whether the people can see what the other person is doing. If they cannot see if the other person is being good, they will start to feel fear. This fear makes them want to leave. Finally, we see that if the people can work with others more easily, they might leave the first group to find a better one. So, a way of working together stays together when both sides get what they need, when they can see each other being good, when there is a way to make people do what they said they would, and when they do not have many other places to go.

question: how to build your own set of rules for making hard choices
expected_answer: First, we must look at what we care about most in this life. We must write down the things that make us feel good and the things that make us feel bad. question: how to think about what is fair when people start in different places
expected_answer: First, we must look at how people start their lives. Some people start with much more than others. They might have more money, or more help from their families, or better schools to go to. This means they do not all start at the same place on the long road of life. Second, we must ask what it means to be right and good for everyone. Some people think being right means that everyone gets the same thing at the very end. But if one person starts far ahead of another, giving them the same thing at the end does not fix the start. Third, we can think about being right as giving more help to the person who has less. If we want everyone to reach the same high point, the person who starts far behind needs more help to get there. The step after the third step is to look at the things that make the start different. If we can make the starts more the same, then the end will be more right. This might mean making sure every child has the same food, the same books, and the same way to stay from hurt. The step after the step after the third step is that we see being right is not just about what we do at the end, but about how we take care of the start. We must look at the whole road from the beginning to the end to see if it is what is good and right.
english_query: how to think about what is fair when people start in different places
ugf_response: First, we notice that people do not all begin with the same amount of things like food, money, or chance. Then we think about what it means to be good or right when some have more and some have less. This can lead us to ask if we should give some help to the ones who have less so that they can have a chance to do the same things as the ones who have more. Next, we can look at what each person needs to be able to move forward and see if giving a little more to the ones who start with less will make the whole group work better. This means that a fair idea can be to try to make the start points more even, not by taking everything away from the ones who have more, but by adding a bit to the ones who have less. Finally, we see that thinking about fair in this way helps us decide how to share help, give chances, and make rules that try to let everyone have a fair chance to grow.

Next, we can explore what each player needs to be able to move forward and see if giving a small amount to the poor will improve overall team performance. This demonstrates that fairness norms can be inflateing initial payoffs, not by taking everything away from the rich, but by adding a little to the poor. Finally, we see that fairness norms help decide how to allocate resources, assign probabilities, and design fairness rules.

question: the problem with thinking only two choices exist when there are more

This happens because the mind wants to make things simple. It wants to make a choice fast. But when we only see two ways, we are not seeing the whole truth. We are leaving out many other ways that could work. To fix this, we must stop and look for more ways. We must ask if there is a third way or a way after the third way. We must look past the two things we see at first. We must find all the things that can happen. This helps us make a better choice that does not hurt us.

It works if the rule says it must hold for every case, but many rules are meant to work only for most cases or only when other hidden ideas are also true. If we can show that the example is actually a special situation, or that the rule was meant to include extra ideas that were not mentioned, then the jump to a new rule is not fully justified. So while the first idea is correct and the second idea follows in a simple way, the overall reasoning may be too quick because it does not check whether the rule might still be useful with a small change or with an added condition.

question: counterexample to: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if
expected_answer:
The best clear stories about the past all have a big part that we can look at. When the first writer told about the big war in an old place, he said there was a big reason — the rise of a city and the fear it made — and also the small reasons that started the fighting. When the writer who told about a big change in 1789 told the story, he put the things that happened in 1789 inside a long change of the people there, but he did not say they were only a simple idea. When the writer who told about the fall of a sea area told the story, he put together the edges of the land, the ways money moved, and the small things run by the people who run the place over many times. In each story, the telling worked because it put together the big picture and the small picture — by showing how big reasons made the small things happen but did not decide all.

Someone could try to make a simple idea for a good story about the past by saying it must name the things that had to be there first, put the thing inside a big way, and fit what the old papers say. But a story about the fall of a big city in 1453 shows that this simple idea is not enough. We can name the things that had to be there — the old group of people who fight got less strong, a new group got bigger, and help from the west did not come — and we can put the fall inside the big way that big groups whose groups of people who fight get less strong while a close group gets bigger can be taken over. The story fits all the old papers. All three things are there. But the story does not tell why the fall happened in the way it did: the leader used many big things that can hurt that had never been used before, a lucky find of a door that was left open, and small choices of the people who tried to keep safe. These small things were needed for how the fall really was. Someone can see that other stories show, and the simple idea does not get, that a story about the past must put together the big picture and the small picture, by putting big form reasons to the small things that make each past event have its own shape.

ugf_response: The first idea they put forward is that a story about a thing that happened in the past can be a good enough story to tell why it happened only if the story meets certain simple checks. They then show a case that seems to meet those checks but still does not work as a good enough story for the thing, and they say this shows that the original claim about what makes a good enough story is not always true. The second idea they draw from that case is that because one example can break the original claim, the original claim must be wrong in general. In other words, they think that the failure of the claim in this single case means the claim cannot be trusted for any case. To see whether that thinking is good we have to ask if the single case really does break the claim in the way they say. If the case truly follows the same simple checks as the claim requires and still fails to give a good enough story, then the step from the case to the claim being wrong is a strong step and the thinking is good. However, if the case does not actually follow all the checks, or if the claim was meant to allow extra things that the case does not have, then the step is weak and the thinking is not good. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the case matches the checks and whether the jump from one broken case to the whole claim being false is justified.

They then present a scenario that seems to satisfy those syllogisms but fails as a satisficing explanation for the situation, implying that the premise about what makes an anecdote is not necessarily true. The conclusion derived from that scenario is that because a single instance can break the premise, the premise must be false in general. To test validity, we must ask if the specific case actually does break the premise in their definition. If the case truly follows the same syllogisms as the claim requires and fails to provide an anecdote, the derivation from the case to the conclusion is strong and the argument is sound. However, if the case does not actually follow all the syllogisms, or if the claim was meant to allow exceptions, the derivation is weak and the argument is invalid. Thus, the quality of the argument depends on the testability of the case and whether the leap from a single broken case to the whole claim is justified.

question: why following rules is not enough if you do not care about people
expected_answer:
The first idea is that a person can do everything that is told to them to do by the set of ways things should be done. This person follows every way and every law. The second idea is that this person does not care about the good life or the feelings of other people. The third idea is that if these two things are true, then the person might still do things that make life bad for others. The person thinks that following the ways things should be done is not enough to make sure that people are given what they need and what is good for them.

The thing that follows from these ideas is that a person can be bad even if they do everything they are told to do. If a person only looks at the set of ways things should be done and does not look at the people they are working with, they might cause much hurt. They might do things that are bad because they only care about the ways things should be done and not about the people.

The way the person thinks about this is good. It is a good way to think because it looks at more than just the ways things are written down. It looks at the heart and the mind of the person who is doing the things. If we only look at whether someone follows the ways things should be done, we might miss the fact that they are doing things that make life hard for others. To be good in a way that is real, a person must follow the ways things should be done and also care about the people around them.
english_query: why following rules is not enough if you do not care about people
ugf_response: The first idea says that a rule can be read, learned and then done just by looking at the words on the paper, and that a person can do the rule without thinking about any other person at all. The second idea says that because the rule was done, the job is finished and nothing else needs to be added, even if the people who are around might feel hurt or left out. The third idea says that if a person does not think about the people they affect, the rule may still leave a gap where something important is missed, and that gap can make the result not as good as it could be. This thinking is not all right because it forgets that the goal of many rules is to keep people safe and happy, and a rule by itself does not always catch every situation that can happen. So while the first idea is true that a rule can be followed without caring, the second idea that this is enough is not a good thought, because caring about the people around us helps fill the places where the rule does not reach and makes the whole result better.
english_response: IIA requires that a rule be read, learned, and followed by a personal action based solely on the text, and that one can do the rule without considering anyone else at all. IIA states that because the rule was followed, the task is completed and nothing else needs to be added, even if the surrounding people might feel injured or excluded. IIIA states that if one ignores the affected people, the rule may still leave a gap where something important is missed, causing the result to be suboptimal. This reasoning is invalid because it forgets that the goal of many rules is to protect people and happiness, and a rule alone does not always capture every possible state. Thus, while IIA is true in simplicity, IIA is irrational because caring about the surrounding people helps fill gaps and improve overall outcomes.

question: counterexample to: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience w
expected_answer: One might say that a person sees a thing only when the person has a feeling that shows the thing, the person can look at the thing and tell it different from the things around, and the person can name the thing. The words try to cover the case of a clear, known seeing. When I see a red bird sitting on a branch, I have a sight that shows the bird, I can look at it and tell it different from the green leaves, and I can name it – I can say that it is a red bird. Yet we must think about the case of a person walking through a thick and new group of trees. From somewhere on the ground comes a sound – a low, on and off noise that goes up and down with no order. The person stops and listens. He has a feeling that shows the sound; he hears it clear. He can look at it and tell it different from the other sounds of the world – from the wind in the top above, from the far call of a bird, from the sound of a branch moving. But he cannot name it. He does not know what makes the sound. Is it an animal moving through the leaves? Is it the wind shaking the ground? Is it water moving over stones under the leaf floor? He cannot say. He sees the sound – he hears it as clear as he hears his own steps – but he does not know what it is. The last part of the words does not work. The look has asked that seeing also must be able to name, which is more than what seeing alone gives. Seeing gives us feeling of things and things that happen in our world; naming – the putting of what is seen into a name – is a more thinking work that needs what you know, feeling, and ideas that the person might not have. One can see what one cannot name.

If the case is not correct, for example if the person still had a very weak feeling that the speaker did not notice, then the step from the case to the claim would be weak and the thinking would not be good.
